recipes:
Boil Base Ingredients: Place potatoes, peas, and corn in a large pot. Cover with water (about 1 inch above the food) and add salt.
Cook: Cover the pot and bring to a boil, then reduce heat to medium and cook until potatoes are soft (roughly 15 - 20 minutes).
Add Greens: If using spinach, add it to the pot during the last 
2-3 minutes of cooking to steam.
Drain & Mash: Drain most of the water, leaving about 4 tablespoons to keep it moist. Mash the mixture with a potato masher or wooden spoon.
Finish: Stir in the butter and season with black pepper and extra salt if needed.
Serve: Serve hot with meat stew.
substitutes: Add pumpkin leaves; replace peas with beans

recipes:
Boil Base Ingredients: In a large pot, add the potatoes, pre-boiled maize, peas, and salt. Add enough water to cover them and boil until the potatoes are tender (about 20-30 minutes).
Prepare Greens: While the potatoes boil, thoroughly wash the pumpkin leaves and boil them in a separate pot for 5-10 minutes until soft. Blend the leaves into a smooth puree.
Mash: Drain the water from the potatoes and maize mixture. Mash thoroughly using a wooden spoon or potato masher until smooth.
Add Greens: Stir the blended pumpkin leaves into the mashed potatoes. Keep mashing to ensure the green color is evenly incorporated.
Sauté and Mix: In a separate small pan, heat the cooking oil and fry the onions until golden brown. Pour the fried onions (and optional butter/seasoning cube) into the mash. Mix well over low heat for 2-3 minutes.
Serve: Serve hot with a beef stew or traditional green

Boil the Beans: Soak the njahi beans in plenty of water overnight. Drain and rinse them. Boil in a large pot for 1–2 hours until very soft, adding a carrot to the water to reduce gas. Once tender, set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: Heat oil in a large pot. Add the onion and sauté until tender. Add the garlic and ginger, cooking until fragrant and golden.
Create the Base: Stir in the tomato paste, chopped tomatoes, bell pepper, and spices (cumin, garam masala, turmeric). Cover and cook for 5–7 minutes until the tomatoes are cooked down and a thick sauce forms.
Simmer with Beans: Add the boiled njahi beans to the pot, along with some of the bean cooking water or beef stock. Cover and let it simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes to allow the beans to absorb the flavors.
Add Coconut & Finish: Pour in the coconut cream/milk, stir gently to combine, and simmer for another 5–10 minutes on low heat to thicken the sauce.
Garnish: Season with salt, add chopped coriander, and simmer for one last minute.
Serve: Serve hot with white rice, chapati, or mashed bananas/plantains.

Prep the Components: Ensure your njahi and maize are thoroughly boiled until tender.
Sauté Aromatics: Heat oil in a pot (sufuria) over medium heat. Sauté the onions until translucent, then add the garlic, ginger, and curry powder, cooking for about 1 minute until fragrant.
Create the Sauce: Add the diced/blended tomatoes, spices, and beef cube. Cover and cook over low heat until the tomatoes are soft and the oil separates (about 5-10 minutes).
Simmer the Githeri: Add the boiled njahi and maize to the tomato mixture. Stir in potatoes or carrots if using.
Add Water: Add a small amount of water (or broth) to create a light gravy. Simmer for 10–15 minutes, until the vegetables are tender and the sauce thickens.
Finish: Garnish with fresh dhania (coriander) before removing from heat

Harvest and Wash: Harvest only the top young leaves, removing them from the woody stems. Wash them thoroughly in cold water to remove dirt.
Blanch (Remove the Sting): Boil a pot of salted water. Add the nettle leaves and blanch them for 2-3 minutes, or soak in hot water for 10 minutes. The sting becomes inactive once cooked.
Drain and Chop: Remove from the hot water and squeeze out excess water. Chop the leaves finely.
Sauté: Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Sauté the onions until translucent. Add the garlic/ginger paste and tomatoes, cooking until soft.
Cook the Thabai: Add the chopped, blanched nettle leaves to the pan. Season with salt.
Simmer: Stir well and let it cook for 5-10 minutes. If you prefer a creamy sauce, you can add a small amount of milk or cream at this stage.
Serve: Serve hot with mashed potatoes, ugali, or traditional dishes like Githeri.

Wash the Mirore leaves thoroughly 2–3 times under running water to remove all soil.
Boiling: Bring water to a boil in a pot. Add the Mirore leaves and the baking soda (or moo). Boil for 3–5 minutes until tender. The leaves should remain green, so do not overcook.
Sauteing: In a separate pan, heat the cooking oil and fry the onions until golden brown. Add the diced tomatoes and seasoning, cooking until they form a soft paste.
Mixing: Add the boiled Mirore leaves to the tomato mixture. Mix well and let it simmer for 1–2 minutes to absorb the flavors.
Creaming: Pour in the milk and stir gently. Cook for another 1 minute.
Serving: Adjust salt to taste and serve hot with ugali.

Clean the spider plant leaves(Thaka) thoroughly to remove dirt and stems.  Boil the leaves in water for 5-10 minutes to reduce the bitterness. Drain the excess water and set aside. Heat oil in a sufuria (pot). Add the onions and cook until golden brown. Add the diced tomatoes and cook until soft, if using. Add the boiled Thaka/Sagaa into the sufuria and mix well with the onions/tomatoes. Add salt and the milk (or cream) to reduce bitterness and add richness. Let it cook for another 5–10 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the liquids have reduced to a thick sauce.Serve with ugali and meat or other stews.

Place chicken in a large pot with salt, garlic, ginger, and enough water to cover it. Boil for 1–2 hours until tender, or 30–45 minutes in a pressure cooker. In a separate pot, heat oil and sauté onions until soft. Add garlic, ginger, and chili, stirring until aromatic. Add chopped/blended tomatoes, tomato paste, and spices (curry powder/Royco). Cover and cook until tomatoes are soft and mushy.
Add the boiled chicken (and its broth) to the tomato mixture. Stir well and simmer on low heat for 10–15 minutes to allow flavors to meld. Stir in fresh capsicum and coriander (dania). Simmer for another 2 minutes and serve, often with ugali

Wash the dehulled maize and pigeon peas thoroughly until the water runs clear. Combine the maize and peas in a pot. Add water (ensure it is enough to cover the mixture) and boil until tender. This takes approximately 1 hour or more, or 30–40 minutes in a pressure cooker. In a separate large pot, heat the cooking oil and add onions, cooking until soft and golden. Add garlic and ginger, then add the diced tomatoes and spices (paprika, turmeric, cumin). Cook the tomato mixture until it forms a thick, consistent sauce and the oil separates. Pour the boiled muthokoi and mbaazi into the sauce and stir well. Add a little water to create a light stew if desired.
Let it simmer for 5–10 minutes on low heat to allow the flavors to blend. Garnish with chopped coriander. Serve hot

Rinse the soaked green grams, place them in a pot with water, and boil for 45-60 minutes until soft and tender. Drain, but keep the broth to use in the stew. Heat oil in a pan, add onions and cook until soft and lightly browned. Add the minced garlic and ginger, frying until fragrant. Add the diced tomatoes, carrots, and spices (curry, turmeric, cumin). Cover and cook until the tomatoes are soft, mushy, and turn into a thick sauce. Add the boiled green grams to the tomato mixture. Stir to coat evenly. Add a cup of the reserved boiled broth (or water) for a light stew, or a few tablespoons for a thick curry. Cover and simmer for 5-10 minutes to allow flavors to blend.
Pour in the coconut milk, stir, and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Stir in fresh coriander (dhania) and remove from heat.

Peel the brown skin off the nduma using a sharp knife and wash them thoroughly. Place them in a pot and add enough water to cover them halfway. Add salt to taste. Cover the pot and boil on high heat for 20-30 minutes, or until they are fork-tender and powdery.
Serve hot with milk tea.

Carefully peel the potatoes and slice them into halves, wash them clean and set aside. Prepare the pumpkin leaves by removing the stalk and shred them into sizeable pieces. You can also blend them for richer colour. lf you are using spinach you just need to shred a little bit as well. Mix the pumpkin leaves, maize and potatoes in a Sufuria and add some water. Just enough for everything to cook well. Place the Sufuria or cooking pot on fire and let the mixture boil for 30 minutes under medium heat. Open and add a pinch of salt and let it cook for 10 more minutes. Reduce the heat and mash everything together using a wooden spoon until all blends in well. Remove from. Heat and set aside. In another larger pot/Sufuria, pour in the spring onions and cooking oil and place on fire. Mix until the onions turn golden brown then adjust the heat to minimal and add the Mashed mixture. Mix well. You can now remove from the heat and serve while hot.

Blanch the spider plant leaves by boiling them for 5-10 minutes to reduce the bitterness, then drain and set aside. In a large pot, heat the cooking oil and sauté the onions until soft and translucent. Add garlic if using. Add the diced tomatoes and carrots. Cook until the tomatoes are tender and form a thick sauce. Add the pre-boiled maize and beans to the tomato mixture. Stir well to coat.
Add a little water (or milk) to create a light sauce. Cover and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes on low heat to allow the flavors to blend.
Stir in the blanched spider plant leaves. Cook for another 3–5 minutes until heated through and well incorporated.
Season with salt and serve hot

Mix 1–1.5 cups of sorghum flour with cold water to form a smooth, lump-free paste. Bring 3–4 cups of water to a boil in a sufuria (pot).
Stir the paste into the boiling water, stirring continuously until it thickens. Reduce heat and simmer for 5–15 minutes. Add milk, sugar, honey, lemon juice, or peanut butter to taste

Peel the green bananas and immediately soak them in cold water to prevent browning. Slice them into medium-sized chunks.Heat the vegetable oil in a pot. Add the onions and cook until soft and translucent. Add the garlic/ginger paste and cook for one minute until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes: Add the chopped tomatoes and tomato paste, cooking until the tomatoes are tender and form a thick base.
Add Spices: Add the curry powder or turmeric and stir well.
Cook the Marigu: Drain the bananas and add them to the pot along with the diced potatoes. Stir to coat them in the mixture.
Simmer: Add enough water (or coconut milk for a richer flavor) to just cover the ingredients. Season with salt, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes or until the bananas and potatoes are tender and the sauce is thick.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander (dania) and serve hot.

Heat oil in a pan, add the onion and sauté until golden brown.
Add Tomatoes: Add the tomatoes and cook until soft and mushy.
Cook Nderema: Add the chopped nderema and stir until it begins to wilt.
Season: Add salt and stir well. Cover and let it cook for 3-5 minutes on low heat.
Add Dairy: Stir in the milk/cream to create a creamy sauce and cook for another 1-2 minutes until heated through.
Serve: Serve with hot ugali.

If using dry pigeon peas, soak them overnight. Boil the pigeon peas and maize together in a pressure cooker or pot until soft and thoroughly cooked. Heat oil in a large pot (sufuria). Add onions and fry until translucent or golden brown. Add garlic and ginger, cooking for 1 minute until fragrant. Add tomatoes, salt, and spices (curry powder). Cook until the tomatoes are mashed and form a thick sauce.  Add the boiled maize and pigeon peas into the sauce. Mix thoroughly, adding a small amount of water or coconut milk to create a light stew if desired. Cover and simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes, allowing flavors to blend. Stir in chopped fresh coriander (dhania). Serve Enjoy hot, best served with avocado or roasted potatoes.

Prepare the Vegetables: Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a pot. Sauté the onions until golden brown. Add the garlic and chili if using.
Add Veggies: Add the chopped tomatoes and carrots. Cook until the tomatoes are well mashed and the carrots are softening. Add the spinach or sukuma wiki, cover, and let it steam for 5 minutes until tender-crisp.
Make the Sorghum Mix: In a separate large pot, bring 2 cups of water to a boil.
Incorporate the Flour: Reduce heat to low. Slowly sprinkle the sorghum flour into the boiling water, stirring continuously with a mwiko (wooden spoon) to prevent lumps.
Combine: Mix the cooked vegetables into the sorghum mixture and stir well to combine. If it is too thick, add a small amount of water to achieve the desired consistency.
Simmer: Cover and cook on low heat for 5–7 minutes, allowing the flavors to mix.

Rinse the soaked green grams, place them in a pot with water, and boil for 45-60 minutes until soft and tender. Drain, but keep the broth to use in the stew. Heat oil in a pan, add onions and cook until soft and lightly browned. Add the minced garlic and ginger, frying until fragrant. Add the diced tomatoes, carrots, and spices (curry, turmeric, cumin). Cover and cook until the tomatoes are soft, mushy, and turn into a thick sauce. Add the boiled green grams to the tomato mixture. Stir to coat evenly. Add a cup of the reserved boiled broth (or water) for a light stew, or a few tablespoons for a thick curry. Cover and simmer for 5-10 minutes to allow flavors to blend.
Pour in the coconut milk, stir, and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Stir in fresh coriander (dhania) and remove from heat.

Prep the Leaves: Wash the cowpea leaves thoroughly to remove sand. Chop them into small pieces.
Boil the Leaves: Bring water to a boil, add a little salt and baking soda (if using). Add the leaves and boil for about 10-15 minutes until tender. Drain the leaves but reserve a cup of the boiled water.
Prepare the Paste: Mix the groundnut powder with a little water or the reserved leaf water to form a smooth paste.
Sauté Aromatics: In a pot, heat the oil and sauté the onions until soft and translucent (add garlic/ginger here if desired). Add the tomatoes and cook until they form a soft paste.
Combine and Simmer: Add the boiled leaves into the tomato mix. Stir well. Pour the groundnut paste over the leaves and stir, ensuring everything is well coated.
Simmer: Lower the heat, cover, and let it simmer for 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the peanut sauce is cooked and thick.
Serve: Serve hot with ugali.

Boil nzuu until soft. Sauté onions, garlic, and ginger. Stir in fresh tomatoes, turmeric, curry powder, and salt. Add coconut milk and simmer until the stew thickens.
Serving: Ideal with chapati, rice, or mahamri.

Boil dry maize and beans together until tender.
In a separate pot, sauté onions, garlic, and ginger.
Add tomatoes and spices, cooking until they form a thick sauce.
Add the boiled maize and beans to the mixture.
Simmer, allowing the flavors to combine

Clean the octopus by removing the eyes and beak, and cleaning the head. In a pressured cooker, boil the octopus with some salt for 20-25 minutes until very tender. Alternatively, simmer for longer (45-60 mins) until soft.
Cut & Fry (Optional): Slice the tender octopus into strips. You can directly add them to the curry or deep-fry them until golden/crispy for a better texture.
Make the Sauce: In a pot, heat oil and sauté onions until golden. Add garlic, ginger, curry powder, and turmeric. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes: Add chopped/grated tomatoes and tomato paste, cooking until they form a thick paste and the oil separates.
Simmer with Coconut: Add the octopus strips into the sauce. Pour in the coconut cream and stir well. Reduce heat and simmer for 10–15 minutes until the sauce is thick and flavorful.
Finishing: Add fresh lime juice, salt to taste, and coriander/cilantro (optional) before serving

Marinate: Mix chicken with all marinade ingredients, let it sit for at least 1 hour, or overnight in the fridge for deep flavor.
Grill/Sear: Light a charcoal grill (jiko) or sear the chicken on a pan over high heat until golden brown and charred on both sides (about 5-10 minutes). Set aside.
Prepare Sauce: In a pot, fry onions until soft. Add garlic, ginger, chilies, tomatoes, and spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric). Cook until the tomatoes are soft and the oil separates.
Simmer: Pour in the coconut milk, bring to a simmer, and stir until it begins to thicken.
Combine: Place the charred chicken pieces into the coconut sauce. Cover and cook on low heat for 10-15 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce is thick.
Finish: Stir in fresh lemon juice and coriander before removing from heat.

Prepare the Mbaazi: If using dry, soak the pigeon peas overnight to soften. Boil them until tender (or use fresh/boiled ones).
Sauté Aromatics: In a large pot, heat oil over medium heat. Add the sliced onions and fry until soft. Add the ginger-garlic paste, diced green pepper, and green chilies. Sauté for another 2 minutes.
Add Spices: Add the turmeric, cumin, and cardamom powders. Stir continuously for 1-2 minutes to cook the spices without burning them.
Simmer with Coconut Milk: Add the boiled mbaazi and salt. Pour in the light coconut milk. Cover the pot and simmer over low-medium heat for about 10–15 minutes until the coconut milk reduces and the flavors are absorbed.
Add Thick Cream: Pour the thick coconut cream over the top. Stir gently to incorporate, then cover and cook for another 5–8 minutes on low heat until the sauce is very thick and creamy.
Serve: Serve hot with Mahamri or Chapati.

Peel the green bananas and wash them. You can cut them into thick chunks or slice them in half lengthwise.
Sauté Onions: Heat oil in a pot and lightly sauté the sliced onions until soft and translucent (not browned).
Add Bananas: Add the bananas to the onions and gently stir for 1-2 minutes to coat them with the oil.
Add Coconut Milk & Seasonings: Pour in the thick coconut milk (or the thin milk if using both, leaving thick for last). Add salt and the optional turmeric/cardamom.
Simmer: Simmer on low heat for 20-25 minutes, or until the bananas are tender and the coconut sauce thickens, coating the bananas properly. Do not rush this process; cooking on low heat ensures the bananas are tender and the sauce is creamy.
Finish: If the sauce is too thick before the bananas are ready, add a little water or thin coconut milk. If you want a more intense coconut flavor, add an extra tablespoon of coconut cream right before removing from heat. Add a squeeze of lemon juice if desired.
Serve: Serve warm as a side dish or main meal, often paired with fish or chicken

In a pot, roast the vermicelli with a tablespoon of ghee or oil until it turns a light golden brown to add a nutty flavor.
Boil: Add the thick coconut milk, cardamom, and sugar to the pot.
Simmer: Reduce heat and let it simmer until the vermicelli is soft and has absorbed most of the coconut milk, forming a thick, creamy sauce.
Serve: Serve hot as a breakfast or dessert

Thoroughly wash the cassava leaves and pound them using a mortar and pestle or blend them until fine.
Boil Leaves: Place the pounded leaves in a large pot, cover with water, and bring to a boil. Add the bicarbonate of soda (optional) to make them tender. Cover and simmer until soft.
Add Seasoning: Add the chopped onions and tomatoes. Allow to cook until they are tender.
Add Groundnuts: In a separate cup, mix the groundnut flour with a little water to form a smooth paste, then stir this into the cassava leaves.
Simmer: Continue cooking on low heat, allowing the stew to thicken for 10-15 minutes.
Season and Serve: Add salt to taste. Stir well, then remove from heat

Wash the rice 2-3 times to remove starch, then soak in water for 20-30 minutes, then drain.
Boil: In a pot, bring the thin coconut milk (or water) and salt to a boil. Add the drained rice.
Cook: Cover and simmer on low heat for about 10-15 minutes until the liquid is mostly absorbed.
Coconut Cream: Pour the thick coconut cream over the rice, gently fluffing it, then cover tightly again and steam on very low heat for another 10 minutes until the rice is tender and creamy.
Rest: Remove from heat and let it rest covered for 5-10 minutes before serving

Mix marinade ingredients, rub onto the fish (including slits), and let it sit for 20-30 minutes. Grill on a hot charcoal grill (or bake at 200°C) for 10-15 minutes per side until cooked and slightly charred.
Make Sauce: Sauté onions, garlic, and ginger until soft. Add turmeric, curry powder, and chili, stirring to avoid burning. Pour in the coconut milk and tamarind paste. Simmer on low heat until the sauce thickens.
Combine: You can either dip the grilled fish into the sauce, or pour the sauce over the fish and place it back on the grill briefly for a smoky flavor.
Serve: Serve hot with rice, chapati, or ugali

Soak and Blend: Drain the soaked rice and blend it with the thick coconut milk until smooth.
Combine & Ferment: Add sugar, cardamom, yeast, and egg (if using) into the blender and blend again until combined. Pour into a bowl, cover, and let it rise for 1–2 hours until frothy.
Cook: Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat and lightly grease with oil or ghee.
Fry: Pour a ladleful of the batter (about 
 cup) into the pan. Cook until tiny holes appear on top and the edges are dry, then flip and cook until golden brown.
Serve: Serve hot with tea or coffee

Prepare the Base: Drain the soaked rice and blend it with the coconut milk, sugar, cardamom, and egg (if using) until the mixture is very smooth.
Rise: Sprinkle the yeast into the batter, mix well, cover the bowl, and let it rise in a warm place for 30 minutes to 1 hour.
Prepare Pan: Grease a baking pan (or a heavy-bottomed frying pan) with ghee.
Cook (Stovetop Method): Pour the batter into the pan. Place on low-medium heat on the stove, cover, and cook for 10-15 minutes until the bottom is set and small holes appear on top.
Bake: Transfer the pan to a preheated oven at 200°C for 25-30 minutes, or until the top is golden brown.
Finish: Remove from the oven, allow it to cool down completely before slicing to prevent it from breaking

Place the meat, salt, and water in a large heavy-bottomed pot (sufuria).
Bring to a boil over high heat, then cover and reduce heat to low.
Simmer for 1–2 hours, adding water only if necessary, until the meat is very tender.
Once the meat is cooked and the water has evaporated, let the meat sear in its own rendered fat for a few minutes.
Serve hot with ugali

Prep the Greens: Pluck the leafy greens from the stalks. Wash them thoroughly in several changes of water to ensure no dirt or sand remains.
Blanching (Optional but Recommended): If the greens are very bitter, boil them in a pot with a little water for 5-10 minutes. Drain the water and set the greens aside.
Sauté Onions: Heat the vegetable oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the diced onions and sauté until they are soft and fragrant, about 3-5 minutes.
Add Aromatics & Tomatoes: Add the garlic/ginger paste (if using) and cook for 1 minute. Add the diced tomatoes and cook until they become soft and create a paste.
Cook the Greens: Add the Lokot (blanched or fresh) into the pan. Stir well to mix with the tomato/onion mixture.
Simmer: Cover the pot and let the greens simmer for 5-10 minutes, allowing them to soften. If they appear too dry, add a tiny amount of water or vegetable broth.
Add Cream/Milk: If you want to make them creamy, add the cream or milk now and stir well. Let it simmer uncovered for an additional 2-3 minutes to allow the liquids to evaporate.
Season: Add salt and the bouillon cube (if using), stir well, and remove from heat.

Mix 1–1.5 cups of sorghum flour with cold water to form a smooth, lump-free paste. Bring 3–4 cups of water to a boil in a sufuria (pot).
Stir the paste into the boiling water, stirring continuously until it thickens. Reduce heat and simmer for 5–15 minutes. Add milk, sugar, honey, lemon juice, or peanut butter to taste

Prepare the Mixture: In a bowl, mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water (or milk) to create a smooth, lump-free paste. Add the lemon juice here if you want a fermented flavor.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining water (approx. 3-4 cups) to a boil in a thick-bottomed pot.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour paste into the boiling water while continuously stirring with a wooden spoon or stick (mwiko) to prevent lumps.
Cook: Reduce heat to low. Continue stirring until it thickens (about 3-5 minutes).
Add Milk & Sweeten: Pour in the milk, stir continuously, and let it simmer for another 5 minutes. Add sugar and a pinch of salt to taste.
Serve: Serve hot in a cup or mug

Preparation: Slice the meat into very thin, long strips to allow for proper drying.
Salting: Rub the salt onto the meat strips thoroughly to reduce moisture and act as a preservative.
Drying: Hang the strips to dry in the sun for 2 to 3 days until they are dehydrated (Aliya style).
Deep-Frying: In a large, heavy-bottomed sufuria, heat the cooking oil to about 100°C.
Cooking: Add the dried meat strips in batches. Deep-fry for approximately 30 minutes, adding the cardamom powder during the process to infuse flavor.
Finishing: Remove from the oil when the meat is tender but fully dehydrated (koche is typically dense and intensely flavored).
Cooling: Allow the koche to cool down completely before serving or storing.

Prepare the Mixture: In a large bowl, mix the sorghum flour, white/rice flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Gradually add warm water while stirring to avoid lumps. The batter should have a smooth, thin consistency, similar to pancake batter.
Fermentation (Crucial Step): Cover the bowl with a clean cloth or plastic wrap and leave it to ferment in a warm place for 14 to 24 hours. The batter is ready when it is bubbly and has a pleasantly tangy (sour) smell.
Tip: In warm Kenya weather, 12-16 hours might be sufficient.
Prep for Cooking: If the mixture is too thick after fermentation, add a tiny amount of water to bring it back to a pourable, thin consistency.
Cooking:
Heat a flat cast-iron skillet or non-stick pancake pan over medium heat.
Lightly grease the pan with a tiny amount of oil.
Pour a ladleful of the batter onto the center of the pan, spreading it quickly in a circular motion to make a thin, flat pancake.
Do not flip. Cover the skillet immediately with a lid and cook for about 1-2 minutes. The steam will cook the top, creating the characteristic "eyes" (small holes) and a spongy texture.
Remove and Cool: Gently remove the cooked injera and let it cool on a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter
Drink fresh or fermented

Prep & Tenderize: Clean the camel meat and, if desired, marinate with turmeric, ginger-garlic paste, salt, and vinegar for 15 minutes.
Pressure Cook: Add the meat to a pressure cooker with a little water (or beef stock) and spices (ginger, garlic, turmeric, peppercorns, cloves). Cook for 2-3 whistles or roughly 30–40 minutes until the meat is tender. Note: Camel meat is leaner and tougher than beef, so tenderizing is essential.
Prepare the Roast Base: In a large pan, heat oil and sauté onions until soft and light brown, then add garlic and tomatoes, cooking until a paste forms.
Roast/Dry Fry: Add the tenderized meat to the pan with the sautéed onions and spices. Stir well and cook on medium heat until the gravy evaporates, coating the meat, and it begins to dry fry, about 20 minutes.
Serve: Add curry leaves and extra black pepper for a "pepper roast" finish, then serve hot with rice, chapati, or roast potatoes

Boil the Meat: Place the meat in a large pot (sufuria), cover with water, and add salt and black pepper. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and cook until the meat is very tender (about 1–2 hours, depending on the meat).
Add Aromatics: Add the chopped onions and ginger-garlic paste to the boiling meat. Let it cook further until the onions soften.
Simmer: Simmer on low heat to allow the flavors to blend. For a more intense soup, you can briefly pan-fry the meat before boiling.
Finish and Serve: Add fresh coriander (dhania) if desired. Serve hot on its own, with Ugali, or with bread
Ferment naturally

Rinse the soaked green grams, place them in a pot with water, and boil for 45-60 minutes until soft and tender. Drain, but keep the broth to use in the stew. Heat oil in a pan, add onions and cook until soft and lightly browned. Add the minced garlic and ginger, frying until fragrant. Add the diced tomatoes, carrots, and spices (curry, turmeric, cumin). Cover and cook until the tomatoes are soft, mushy, and turn into a thick sauce. Add the boiled green grams to the tomato mixture. Stir to coat evenly. Add a cup of the reserved boiled broth (or water) for a light stew, or a few tablespoons for a thick curry. Cover and simmer for 5-10 minutes to allow flavors to blend.
Pour in the coconut milk, stir, and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Stir in fresh coriander (dhania) and remove from heat.
substitutes: Mash lightly or fry with onions

recipes:
Boil the Meat: Boil the cleaned dried meat in water until soft and tender (approx. 1–2 hours or 1 hour in a pressure cooker).
Sauté: In a separate large pot, heat cooking oil (or ghee) and fry the onions until golden.
Create Stew: Add tomatoes and cook until they break down into a paste.
Combine: Add the boiled meat to the tomato mixture. Add in the fresh Kunde (cowpea leaves).
Cook Down: Stir well and let it cook for about 5–10 minutes to allow the leaves to wilt and absorb the broth.
Optional Creamy Finish: Stir in a cup of milk and simmer for an additional 1–2 minutes for a creamy sauce.
Serve: Best served hot with ugali

Prepare the Mixture: In a bowl, mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water to create a smooth, lump-free paste. Add the lemon juice here if you want a fermented flavor.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining water (approx. 3-4 cups) to a boil in a thick-bottomed pot.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour paste into the boiling water while continuously stirring with a wooden spoon or stick (mwiko) to prevent lumps.
Cook: Reduce heat to low. Continue stirring until it thickens (about 3-5 minutes).
Add Milk & Sweeten: Pour in the milk, stir continuously, and let it simmer for another 5 minutes. Add sugar and a pinch of salt to taste.
Serve: Serve hot in a cup or mug

Peel the green bananas and immediately soak them in cold water to prevent browning. Slice them into medium-sized chunks.Heat the vegetable oil in a pot. Add the onions and cook until soft and translucent. Add the garlic/ginger paste and cook for one minute until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes: Add the chopped tomatoes and tomato paste, cooking until the tomatoes are tender and form a thick base.
Add Spices: Add the curry powder or turmeric and stir well.
Cook the Kitwii: Drain the bananas and add them to the pot along with the diced potatoes. Stir to coat them in the mixture.
Simmer: Add enough water (or coconut milk for a richer flavor) to just cover the ingredients. Season with salt, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes or until the bananas and potatoes are tender and the sauce is thick.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander (dania) and serve hot.

Prepare the Jiko: Light the charcoal jiko and let it burn until the coals are red-hot and covered with a light layer of ash.
Clean the Maize: Remove the husks and silk from the maize.
Roast the Maize: Place the maize directly onto the grill over the hot coals. Turn the maize frequently to ensure even roasting and to achieve a consistent golden-brown color without burning.
Prepare the Seasoning: Mix the salt and chilli powder (if using) in a small container.
Apply Flavor: Once the maize is fully cooked and slightly charred, dip the halved lemon/lime into the salt-chili mixture and rub it thoroughly over the hot maize.
Serve: Serve immediately while hot.

Prepare Vegetables: Thoroughly wash the vegetables and remove them from their stalks.
Cook Veggies (Optional): Sauté onions in a pot and cook the greens until soft, or blanch them.
Boil Water: In a separate pot, bring the 3–4 cups of water to a boil.
Add Flour: Add a small amount of millet flour (2-3 tablespoons) to the boiling water, stirring to form a light porridge to prevent lumps.
Cook Thick: Gradually add the remaining flour while stirring continuously with a mwiko (wooden spoon).
Simmer: Cover and cook on low heat for 5-10 minutes, stirring occasionally until it is thick and smooth.
Combine: Mix the cooked vegetables into the millet dough, or serve it on the side.

Prep the Leaves: Wash leaves thoroughly. Snap the stems and pull off the fibrous, tough outer string, then chop the leaves and tender stems.
Boil/Blanch: Boil the chopped leaves in a little water for 3–5 minutes until they wilt. Drain the excess water.
Sauté Aromatics: In a pan, heat oil over medium heat. Add onions and cook until golden brown. Add tomatoes and cook until they are soft and form a sauce.
Combine & Cream: Add the pre-boiled leaves to the onions/tomatoes, stirring well. Season with salt or Royco.
Add Milk: Pour in the milk, cream, or coconut cream. Simmer for another 2–5 minutes on low heat until the sauce thickens and the leaves are tender.
Serve: Serve hot with Ugali, rice, or side dishes

Preparation: Clean the termites thoroughly, washing them multiple times to remove sand and soil. Remove the wings, often by lightly roasting them first or shaking them in a container until wings fall off.
Cooking: Place the cleaned termites in a dry sufuria (cooking pot) over medium heat. Do not add oil, as termites contain natural oils.
Seasoning: Add salt to taste.
Frying: Stir continuously until they are dry, crunchy, and turn a light brown color.

Boil the tsimbande until very tender.
Use a traditional stirrer (mwiko) to add maize flour into the boiling nuts gradually.
Mash and stir until the flour is cooked and the mixture is thick and smooth.

Boil the cowpea leaves for 5–10 minutes until soft. Drain excess water.
Heat oil in a pan, sauté onions until soft, and add tomatoes.
Add the boiled leaves, salt, and garlic/spices. Stir well.
Cook for another 3–5 minutes until the tomatoes form a thick sauce.

Prepare the Mixture: In a bowl, mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water to create a smooth, lump-free paste. Add the lemon juice here if you want a fermented flavor.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining water (approx. 3-4 cups) to a boil in a thick-bottomed pot.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour paste into the boiling water while continuously stirring with a wooden spoon or stick (mwiko) to prevent lumps.
Cook: Reduce heat to low. Continue stirring until it thickens (about 3-5 minutes).
Add Milk & Sweeten: Pour in the milk, stir continuously, and let it simmer for another 5 minutes. Add sugar and a pinch of salt to taste.
Serve: Serve hot in a cup or mug
Ferment milk naturally

Place chicken in a large pot with salt, garlic, ginger, and enough water to cover it. Boil for 1–2 hours until tender, or 30–45 minutes in a pressure cooker. In a separate pot, heat oil and sauté onions until soft. Add garlic, ginger, and chili, stirring until aromatic. Add chopped/blended tomatoes, tomato paste, and spices (curry powder/Royco). Cover and cook until tomatoes are soft and mushy.
Add the boiled chicken (and its broth) to the tomato mixture. Stir well and simmer on low heat for 10–15 minutes to allow flavors to meld. Stir in fresh capsicum and coriander (dania). Simmer for another 2 minutes and serve, often with ugali

Peel the green bananas and immediately soak them in cold water to prevent browning. Slice them into medium-sized chunks.Heat the vegetable oil in a pot. Add the onions and cook until soft and translucent. Add the garlic/ginger paste and cook for one minute until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes: Add the chopped tomatoes and tomato paste, cooking until the tomatoes are tender and form a thick base.
Add Spices: Add the curry powder or turmeric and stir well.
Cook the Bananas: Drain the bananas and add them to the pot along with the diced potatoes. Stir to coat them in the mixture.
Simmer: Add enough water (or coconut milk for a richer flavor) to just cover the ingredients. Season with salt, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes or until the bananas and potatoes are tender and the sauce is thick.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander (dania) and serve hot.

Clean the spider plant leaves(Chinsaga) thoroughly to remove dirt and stems.  Boil the leaves in water for 5-10 minutes to reduce the bitterness. Drain the excess water and set aside. Heat oil in a sufuria (pot). Add the onions and cook until golden brown. Add the diced tomatoes and cook until soft, if using. Add the boiled Thaka/Sagaa into the sufuria and mix well with the onions/tomatoes. Add salt and the milk (or cream) to reduce bitterness and add richness. Let it cook for another 5–10 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the liquids have reduced to a thick sauce.Serve with ugali and meat or other stews.

Mix 1–1.5 cups of sorghum flour with cold water to form a smooth, lump-free paste. Bring 3–4 cups of water to a boil in a sufuria (pot).
Stir the paste into the boiling water, stirring continuously until it thickens. Reduce heat and simmer for 5–15 minutes. Add milk, sugar, honey, lemon juice, or peanut butter to taste

Prepare the Pumpkin: Peel the pumpkin, remove the seeds, and cut it into small cubes.
Boil the Pumpkin: Place the pumpkin cubes in a large pot (sufuria) and add enough water to cover them. Bring to a boil until the pumpkin is very soft.
Mash: Once the pumpkin is soft, use a wooden spoon or potato masher to mash it completely into a smooth paste.
Add the Maize:
If using whole boiled maize: Add it to the mashed pumpkin.
If using maize flour: Add the flour gradually while stirring to avoid lumps, cooking it like you would porridge or a thick mash.
Simmer: Cover the pot and allow the mixture to simmer on low heat for about 10–15 minutes, allowing the flavors to mix.
Seasoning: Add salt to taste. If you prefer it as a sweet dish, you can add a little sugar and a splash of milk at this stage.
Serve: Serve hot. It is often eaten as a main meal or as a snack

Peel and Clean: Use a sharp knife to cut the cassava into manageable chunks (3–4 inches). Peel off the thick brown skin and the thin pinkish layer underneath. Wash the pieces thoroughly in clean water to remove soil.
Remove Core: Slice the cassava pieces in half and remove the tough fibrous center string.
Boil: Place the cassava in a large pot (sufuria) and add enough water to cover them completely. Add salt.
Cook: Cover the pot tightly to keep steam inside and boil over medium-high heat for 20 to 30 minutes, or until the cassava is soft and tender.
Drain and Serve: Once soft, drain any remaining water, and serve immediately.

Prepare the Meat: Rinse the goat meat, pat it dry, and cut it into large, manageable chunks. Make shallow, 2-inch slits across the meat to allow flavors to penetrate.
Marinate: In a small bowl, mix the garlic, lemon juice, oil, and spices (if using). Rub this marinade all over the meat, ensuring it enters the slits.
Marination Time: Cover the meat and let it marinate in the fridge for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight for maximum flavor.
Prepare the Grill: Preheat your barbecue grill to medium-low heat.
Grill/Roast: Place the meat on the grill. The secret to good nyama choma is "low and slow"—cooking over low heat, away from direct flames, for about 1 to 1.5 hours.
Baste: Turn the meat every 15-20 minutes. Baste with oil or water to keep it moist.
Serve: The meat is ready when it is nicely charred on the outside and tender on the inside. Slice into smaller, bite-sized pieces and serve with kachumbari (tomato-onion salad) and ugali

Prepare the Mixture: In a bowl, mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water to create a smooth, lump-free paste. Add the lemon juice here if you want a fermented flavor.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining water (approx. 3-4 cups) to a boil in a thick-bottomed pot.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour paste into the boiling water while continuously stirring with a wooden spoon or stick (mwiko) to prevent lumps.
Cook: Reduce heat to low. Continue stirring until it thickens (about 3-5 minutes).
Add Milk & Sweeten: Pour in the milk, stir continuously, and let it simmer for another 5 minutes. Add sugar and a pinch of salt to taste.
Serve: Serve hot in a cup or mug

Boil: Heat the milk until it boils, then let it simmer for a few minutes (optional: this reduces water content for a thicker result).
Cool: Let the milk cool to lukewarm (around 30–40°C).
Add Culture: Stir in the starter culture (previously fermented milk or yogurt).
Ferment: Pour the mixture into a container with a lid (a thermos is excellent). Keep it in a warm, dark place undisturbed for 12-24 hours.
Serve: The milk will become thick, tangy, and sour. Shake well and keep in the fridge

Prep the Leaves: Wash leaves thoroughly. Snap the stems and pull off the fibrous, tough outer string, then chop the leaves and tender stems.
Boil/Blanch: Boil the chopped leaves in a little water for 3–5 minutes until they wilt. Drain the excess water.
Sauté Aromatics: In a pan, heat oil over medium heat. Add onions and cook until golden brown. Add tomatoes and cook until they are soft and form a sauce.
Combine & Cream: Add the pre-boiled leaves to the onions/tomatoes, stirring well. Season with salt or Royco.
Add Milk: Pour in the milk, cream, or coconut cream. Simmer for another 2–5 minutes on low heat until the sauce thickens and the leaves are tender.
Serve: Serve hot with Ugali, rice, or side dishes

Prepare the Mixture: In a bowl, mix the millet flour with 1 cup of cold water (or milk) to create a smooth, lump-free paste. Add the lemon juice here if you want a fermented flavor.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining water (approx. 3-4 cups) to a boil in a thick-bottomed pot.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour paste into the boiling water while continuously stirring with a wooden spoon or stick (mwiko) to prevent lumps.
Cook: Reduce heat to low. Continue stirring until it thickens (about 3-5 minutes).
Add Milk & Sweeten: Pour in the milk, stir continuously, and let it simmer for another 5 minutes. Add sugar and a pinch of salt to taste.
Serve: Serve hot in a cup or mug

Boil the Beans: Boil the beans until tender. If you prefer a faster method, you can use a pressure cooker.
Sauté Onions: Heat the oil in a sufuria (pot) and add the onions. Sauté until they turn golden brown.
Add Aromatics: Stir in the garlic and ginger paste, cooking for about 1 minute until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes and Veggies: Add the tomatoes, grated carrots, curry powder, and salt. Cook on medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes are soft and form a thick paste.
Add Beans: Pour in the boiled beans and mix thoroughly with the tomato mixture to ensure they are well-coated.
Simmer: Add 1 cup of water or broth to create the stew consistency. Cover the pot and let it simmer on low heat for about 10–15 minutes, allowing the flavours to blend and the sauce to thicken.
Garnish: Stir in the fresh coriander (dania) and remove from heat.

Boil the cowpea leaves for 5–10 minutes until soft. Drain excess water.
Heat oil in a pan, sauté onions until soft, and add tomatoes.
Add the boiled leaves, salt, and garlic/spices. Stir well.
Cook for another 3–5 minutes until the tomatoes form a thick sauce.

Gourd Preparation (Crucial Step):
Clean the interior of the gourd, removing seeds, using the sosiot stick.
Take branches of the Itet tree, burn them until they produce glowing, soot-rich charcoal.
Insert the burning, smoldering charcoal into the gourd.
Rotate the gourd to ensure the entire inside is coated with fine black charcoal dust (soot/wosek).
Allow the charcoal to burn out and cool, leaving the aromatic residue inside. This reduces porosity and adds flavor.
Milk Preparation:
Boil the fresh raw milk to sterilize it.
Let the boiled milk cool down completely to room temperature.
Fermentation:
Pour the cooled, boiled milk into the treated gourd.
If using, add a small amount of already fermented Mursik to speed up the process.
Cork the gourd tightly to make it airtight.
Maturation & Storage:
Store the gourd in a cool, dry place for 3 to 7 days to allow it to sour and thicken.
When ready, the milk will be thick, with a sour-smoky taste and small butter globules floating.
Shaking & Serving:
Shake the gourd vigorously to ensure a smooth, uniform consistency before serving.
It is often served with Ugali, or consumed directly from the calabash on special occasions

Prepare the Mixture: In a large jug or traditional calabash, mix the flour with enough lukewarm water to form a smooth, thick paste.
Fermentation: Cover the container with a cloth and store it in a warm, dark place for 2 to 3 days to allow natural fermentation. The mixture will develop a sour smell and white foam, indicating it is ready.
Cooking (Optional - for texture): While it can be consumed raw, many prepare it by boiling it into a thin porridge. Boil about 1 liter of water in a pot.
Combine: Slowly stir the fermented mixture into the boiling water. Continue stirring to prevent lumps and simmer on low heat for 10–15 minutes.
Cooling & Serving: Remove from heat, add more water if it is too thick (it should be drinkable). Allow it to cool, then refrigerate. Serve cold with sugar or lemon to taste.

Prep and Clean: Thoroughly wash the bones. To ensure a clear broth, you can soak them in cold water for a few hours to draw out blood, then drain.
Optional Roasting: For a richer flavor, roast the bones in a hot oven for 20 minutes before boiling.
Initial Boil: Put the bones in a large sufuria (pot) and cover with plenty of water. Bring to a boil and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes, then skim off the scum that rises to the top.
Add Aromatics: Add the onions, garlic, ginger, black pepper, and chilies.
Long Simmer: Cover and let the broth simmer on low heat for 2-4 hours. The goal is to get the marrow to loosen and the collagen to turn into a rich, gelatinous soup.
Tip: If using a pressure cooker, this can take 45–60 minutes.
Seasoning: Add salt to taste in the final 30 minutes of cooking.
Serve: Remove from heat, stir in fresh coriander, and serve hot in mugs or bowls with a squeeze of fresh lemon.

Prepare & Marinate: Wash and pat the meat dry. Mix all marinade ingredients and rub thoroughly into the meat. Let it marinate for at least 2 hours or overnight in the fridge for best flavor.
Preheat & Package: Preheat the oven to 180 -200 degrees celcius. Place the meat on a roasting tray or wire rack, then cover tightly with aluminum foil to ensure tenderizing.
Roast: Roast for 1.5 to 2 hours, checking for tenderness.
Brown: Remove the foil, increase the heat slightly, and roast for an additional 20-30 minutes, flipping once to get a golden-brown, charred exterior.
Rest: Let the meat rest for 10 minutes before serving.

Marinate: In a large bowl, rub the meat with salt, pepper, garlic-ginger paste, lemon juice, and oil. Let it sit for at least 1 hour, or overnight in the fridge for deeper flavor.
Grill Preparation: Set up a charcoal grill (ideal) or a broiler. The key to authentic Nyama Choma is a slow, smoky cook.
Roast: Place the meat on the grill. Turn slowly, allowing each side to develop a charred, smoky crust. Continue cooking for about 30–45 minutes until the meat is succulent but cooked through.
Serve: Chop the meat into smaller bite-size pieces, sprinkle with a little more salt/chili if desired, and serve immediately

Extraction: Fresh blood is collected from a live cow by tightening its neck and using a specialized arrow to make a shallow, precise incision in the jugular vein.
Safety: The technique is designed so that the cow is not killed, allowing it to survive and produce blood again.
Mixing: The fresh, warm blood is directly mixed with fresh raw milk.
Consumption: It is often served immediately, consumed warm, and has a taste described as a mixture of milk and a mineral-rich iron taste.

Boil the Meat: Place beef in a pot with water, salt, and ginger/garlic. Cover and boil until tender and water reduces (roughly 15–20 minutes). If water hasn't reduced, set the broth (stock) aside.
Fry Aromatics: Heat oil in a saucepan (sufuria), add onions, and sauté until golden brown. Add garlic/ginger and cook for 1 minute.
Add Tomatoes & Spices: Add chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, curry powder, and paprika. Cook until the tomatoes are soft and oil separates.
Simmer Stew: Add the boiled beef to the mixture and stir well. Add potatoes, carrots, and the reserved beef stock.
Thicken: Cover and cook on medium heat for 10-15 minutes, or until the vegetables are tender and the gravy is thick.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander (dhania) and serve

Soak: Place bones in a bowl of cold salted water for 2-12 hours in the fridge to remove blood. If you are in a rush, skip this and just wash them.
Preheat: Preheat your oven to 200–230°C (400–450°F).
Prepare & Season: Pat the bones dry. Place them on a baking sheet, marrow-side up. Drizzle with oil and season well with salt and pepper.
Roast: Roast for 15 to 20 minutes until the marrow is soft, slightly browned on top, and pulls away from the bone edge. Do not overcook, or the marrow will melt out.
Serve: Garnish with fresh parsley and a squeeze of lemon juice. Serve immediately on hot toast.

Prepare and Marinate: Wash the meat strips and pat them dry. In a large bowl, mix the beef strips with garlic-ginger paste, soy sauce, black pepper, paprika, and salt. Allow it to marinate for at least 1 hour (up to 24 hours in the fridge for best results).
Boil/Steam (Optional): If the beef is tough, place the marinated meat into a sufuria (pot) with a little water (about 1/2 cup) and bring to a boil until tender and the water evaporates.
Dry Fry: Heat oil in a pan or sufuria over medium-high heat. Add the marinated meat strips and sear them until they are browned on all sides.
Add Aromatics: Add the chopped onions and cook until translucent. Add the green bell pepper and tomatoes (if using) and cook until the tomatoes break down, creating a thick coating on the meat.
Finish: Reduce heat and let it fry until the meat is cooked through and tender, adding a tiny amount of water if necessary to prevent burning.
Garnish: Turn off the heat and garnish with fresh coriander

Prep and Clean: Thoroughly wash the bones. To ensure a clear broth, you can soak them in cold water for a few hours to draw out blood, then drain.
Optional Roasting: For a richer flavor, roast the bones in a hot oven for 20 minutes before boiling.
Initial Boil: Put the bones in a large sufuria (pot) and cover with plenty of water. Bring to a boil and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes, then skim off the scum that rises to the top.
Add Aromatics: Add the onions, garlic, ginger, black pepper, and chilies.
Long Simmer: Cover and let the broth simmer on low heat for 2-4 hours. The goal is to get the marrow to loosen and the collagen to turn into a rich, gelatinous soup.
Tip: If using a pressure cooker, this can take 45–60 minutes.
Seasoning: Add salt to taste in the final 30 minutes of cooking.
Serve: Remove from heat, stir in fresh coriander, and serve hot in mugs or bowls with a squeeze of fresh lemon.

Boil Water: Bring water to a boil in a small pot (sufuria). If using ginger or spices, add them now and let them simmer for 2-3 minutes to release flavor.
Add Tea: Add the tea leaves and allow them to boil for about 1 minute until the water is dark.
Add Milk & Sugar: Pour in the milk and add sugar. Stir well.
Simmer: Bring the mixture to a boil again, allowing it to bubble up (be careful it doesn't overflow). Reduce heat and simmer for 2-5 minutes until it reaches a rich, creamy brown color.
Strain and Serve: Turn off the heat and strain through a fine mesh sieve into cups.

core_ingredients: Green bananas, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic,Ginger
Steps: Prepare the Bananas: Peel the green bananas by cutting off the ends and slicing the skin lengthwise, then soaking them in cold water to prevent browning.
Boil/Steam: Place the peeled bananas in a pot (or traditional banana leaves), cover with water, and boil until tender (about 20-30 minutes).
Prepare the Sauce: In a separate pot, sauté onions in oil until golden. Add garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, and spices (curry, pepper). Cook until the tomatoes are soft and a thick sauce forms.
Combine: Add the cooked bananas to the sauce. If making a stew, add about 1-2 cups of water or broth, cover, and simmer for 10-15 minutes on low heat to allow the bananas to absorb the flavors.
Finish: Mash gently if desired, or serve as a stew. Garnish with fresh coriander.
Time: 40 min

Steps: Boil Water: Bring a pot of water to a boil.
Add Flour: Gradually pour in the maize flour while stirring (or "mingling") continuously to prevent lumps.
Mingle (Stir): Use a wooden spoon to mingle the mixture vigorously. Add more flour if necessary, aiming for a consistency thicker than mashed potatoes.
Cook & Steam: Reduce the heat, cover the pot, and let it simmer for about 10-15 minutes.
Serve: Turn the cooked posho out of the pot onto a serving plate and serve hot with beans, vegetable stew, or meat. 
Time: 15 min

core_ingredients: Dry beans, onion, tomato, cooking oil, salt
recipes:
Steps: Prep the Beans: Soak dried beans overnight, then boil with salt until tender (45 mins - 1 hour). Do not discard the stock.
Sauté Aromatics: Heat oil in a pan, add onions, and fry until golden brown.
Make the Gravy: Add tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and curry powder to the onions. Cook down for 5-10 minutes until the tomatoes are mushy and separated from the oil.
Simmer Stew: Add the cooked beans and 1-2 cups of their stock to the gravy. Season with salt/pepper.
Finish: Simmer gently for 15-20 minutes until the sauce is thick and creamy. Garnish with coriander or green pepper, if desired
Time: 45 min

Method: Stew
Steps: Prepare the Paste: If your groundnut paste is very thick, mix it with a little water in a bowl to create a smooth, runny paste to avoid lumps.
Sauté Veggies: In a medium saucepan, add a small amount of oil (optional, as peanuts are oily) and sauté the onions until soft. Add the tomatoes (and green pepper if using). Cook until they form a paste.
Combine: Pour the groundnut paste into the pan. Stir constantly while gradually adding water or broth to reach your desired consistency.
Simmer: Bring the mixture to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to low.
Cook Patiently: Simmer for 25–40 minutes, stirring frequently to prevent the bottom from burning. The stew is ready when it thickens and a layer of oil starts to float on top.
Season & Serve: Add salt to taste. Serve hot with matooke, rice, or posho
Time: 30 min

core_ingredients: Matoke, beans or meat, cooking oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger
Method: Steam/Stew
Steps: Prep & Sear: Sauté onions, garlic, and ginger in a large pot until golden. Add cubed beef and brown it for a few minutes.
Add Vegetables: Add chopped tomatoes and green pepper to the beef, cooking until the tomatoes are soft.
Combine: Add the peeled green bananas (matooke) to the pot.
Cook: Pour in water until the food is just covered. Add seasoning (curry powder, stock cube).
Simmer: Cover the pot and simmer on low heat, allowing the ingredients to cook and the sauce to thicken until the matooke is soft.
Serve: Serve warm, often with tea or fresh coriander on top
Time: 40 min

core_ingredients: Eggs, chapati, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Method: Fry
Steps: Prepare the Mixture: Finely chop the onions, cabbage, tomatoes, and coriander. In a bowl, whisk the eggs with the onions, cabbage, coriander, salt, and pepper.
Cook the Omelette: Heat 1 tablespoon of oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Pour in the egg mixture, swirling it to cover the pan.
Add Chapati: Before the egg sets completely, place a chapati on top of the egg mixture, pressing down gently to ensure it sticks.
Flip and Cook: Once the egg is cooked through and golden, carefully flip the entire mixture so the chapati is on the bottom to warm through.
Add Fillings & Roll: Remove from the heat. Add sliced tomatoes or avocado in the center, if desired, and roll the chapati tightly into a cylinder.
Serve: Wrap in foil or paper to hold it together and serve hot
Time: 10 min

Method: Boil
Steps: Prepare the Potatoes: Peel the sweet potatoes and immediately place them in a bowl of cold water to prevent them from turning dark. Wash them thoroughly.
Add Water: Place the cleaned sweet potatoes into a saucepan (sufuria). Add water until the potatoes are covered halfway; avoid covering them completely to keep them from losing their sweetness.
Boil/Steam: Cover the pot with a tight-fitting lid to trap steam. Place on high heat and bring to a boil.
Cook: Boil for 20-25 minutes, or until tender when poked with a knife or fork.
Serve: Remove from the water immediately to avoid making them soggy. Serve warm with your favorite sauce, such as g-nut sauce. 
Time: 25–35 min

Method: Boil
Steps:Prepare the Cassava: Score the thick brown skin lengthwise with a knife. Wedge the knife under the skin to peel it off, including the thick white/pinkish layer underneath.
Wash & Cut: Thoroughly wash the peeled tubers to remove dirt. Cut them into 3-4 inch chunks.
Core: Slice each piece in half and remove the hard, fibrous core running through the center.
Boil: Place the pieces into a saucepan, covering them completely with water. Add salt.
Cook: Bring to a boil over high heat, then reduce to medium heat. Cover the pot tightly to keep the steam in. Boil for 20–45 minutes until the cassava is soft and the edges begin to split.
Drain & Serve: Drain the excess water and serve immediately while hot, often accompanied by beef stew or beans. 
Time: 30–45 min

core_ingredients: Fresh fish, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Method: Stew   Steps: Fry onion, tomato, spices → Add fish → Simmer gently
Time: 30 min

Method: Fry
Steps: Prep & Sear Fish: Clean the tilapia and cut into pieces. Lightly fry the fish pieces until golden (optional, but helps keep the fish firm) and set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: In a clean pot, heat oil and sauté onions until translucent. Add the ginger-garlic paste, carrots, and green pepper, cooking for 2–3 minutes.
Make Sauce: Add the tomatoes, curry powder, turmeric, and other spices. Cook covered until the tomatoes are soft and reduced to a paste.
Simmer Stew: Add water or fish stock and bring to a simmer. Gently place the fish into the sauce. Cover and let it simmer on low heat for 10–15 minutes.
Thicken: Mix a little water with Royco or cornstarch to form a paste and stir into the stew. Let it bubble until the sauce is thick.
Finish: Garnish with coriander leaves and turn off the heat. 
Time: 15 min
substitutes: Canned bamboo shoots, Hearts of palm

core_ingredients: Solanum leaves, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Steps:Pluck the green leaves and tender tops from the hard stalks. Wash thoroughly, checking for dirt and bugs.
Chop the leaves into small, manageable pieces.
In a pan, steam the leaves with very little water until they are tender (roughly 5-10 minutes).
Optional: In a separate pot, lightly fry onions and tomatoes in oil, add the steamed nakati, and mix well.
Add salt to taste and let it simmer for another 2-3 minutes.
Serve hot with matooke
Time: 15 min

core_ingredients: Amaranth leaves, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Steps: Prep: Wash the Doodo leaves thoroughly to remove sand. Chop them finely.
Sauté: Heat the oil in a saucepan. Add the onions and cook until translucent.
Add Tomatoes: Add the chopped tomatoes and stir until they are soft and form a paste.
Cook Greens: Add the chopped Doodo and stir well to mix with the tomatoes and onions.
Simmer: Reduce heat, cover, and let it steam for 3–5 minutes until the greens are tender and bright green.
Finish: Add salt to taste. If using groundnut paste, mix it in now and let it simmer for 2 more minutes.
Serve: Serve hot with posho, matooke, or rice. 
Time: 15 min

Spices: Garlic, ginger
Steps: Prep Leaves: Thoroughly wash banana leaves and pass them over an open flame until they are soft and pliable.
Prepare Meat: Brown the meat in a pan (optional, but adds flavor). Marinate the meat with diced onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and spices, leaving some onions and tomatoes for the sauce.
Make G-Nut Sauce: In a separate bowl, mix the groundnut paste with hot water or broth to create a smooth, thick sauce. Add this to the meat mixture.
Wrap: Place the meat and sauce mixture in the center of the banana leaves. Ensure the wrapping is secure, tying the top with strings made from banana fibers or twine.
Steam: Place the wrapped luwombo in a pot (often over a bed of steamer sticks) with a small amount of water at the bottom. Cover tightly and steam for 1.5–2 hours, adding more hot water if necessary.
Serve: Serve hot with steamed matooke, rice, or potatoes
Time: 60–90 min

core_ingredients: Millet flour or sorghum flour, water
recipes:
Method: Boil
Steps: Prepare the Paste: In a bowl, mix the flour with 1/2 cup of cold water. Stir until smooth with no lumps.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining 3-4 cups of water to a boil in a large saucepan.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour mixture into the boiling water while stirring constantly with a wooden spoon to prevent lumping.
Thicken: Continue to stir on medium heat until the porridge thickens and starts to bubble (about 5-10 minutes).
Simmer & Customize: Reduce heat. If you like it thinner, add more water. Stir in sugar to taste. For a richer taste, add milk.
Add Sourness (Optional): Stir in lemon juice or vinegar for a fermented taste.
Finish: Simmer for another 5 minutes.
Serve: Serve hot in a bowl or mug
Time: 10 min

Method: Boil
Steps: Clean: Thoroughly wash the peanuts in their shells to remove all soil.
Soak (Optional): Soak dried peanuts for 1–2 hours to reduce cooking time.
Boil: Place the cleaned peanuts in a large pot (sufuria). Cover with water and add a generous amount of salt.
Cook: Cover the pot and bring to a boil over medium heat. Cook for roughly 45 minutes to 1.5 hours for fresh peanuts, or longer for dried ones, until they are soft and tender.
Check: Check occasionally to ensure the water does not run out, adding more if necessary.
Finish: Drain the water once cooked and let them cool down slightly before serving.
Time: 45–60 min

Method: Boil
Steps: Boil Water: Bring the water to a boil in a heavy pot. Optional: Add a tablespoon of flour to the water to start the consistency.
Add Flour: Gradually add the maize flour while stirring continuously with a wooden spoon to prevent lumps.
Stir & Mash: Keep stirring and pressing the mixture against the sides of the pot to break up lumps.
Cook Through: Reduce heat to medium-low. Keep cooking and stirring until the mixture is thick, smooth, and pulls away from the sides of the pot (about 5–10 minutes).
Shape: Transfer the hot ugali onto a plate, turning it upside down to form a smooth mound, and let it firm up for a minute. 
Time: 15–30 min

Method: Boil
Steps: Wash the rice thoroughly and soak for 15-30 minutes to ensure fluffy grains.
Bring the coconut milk, water, and salt (and cardamom if using) to a boil in a pot.
Add the rice and boil until the liquid is nearly reduced, stirring occasionally.
Reduce heat to low, cover the pot tightly (use foil or a tight lid), and simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until the rice is tender and the liquid is fully absorbed.
Fluff with a fork and serve hot
Time: 15–30 min

Spices: Garlic, ginger
Method: Stew
Steps: Prep the Beans: Soak dried beans in water overnight. Drain, rinse, and boil in fresh water until soft (approx. 45 mins-1 hour, or 3-4 whistles in a pressure cooker).
Sauté Aromatics: Heat oil in a large pan over medium heat. Add onions and fry until soft and translucent.
Add Spices & Tomatoes: Stir in garlic and ginger, cooking for a few seconds until fragrant. Add the tomatoes (or tomato paste), turmeric, cumin/curry powder, and salt. Cook until the tomatoes are mashed and the oil separates.
Simmer with Coconut Milk: Add the boiled beans to the skillet, stirring to coat them in the spices. Pour in the coconut milk, stirring to combine.
Finish: Reduce heat to low and simmer for 10-15 minutes until the sauce thickens and the flavors mingle.
Garnish: Stir in fresh coriander before serving. 
Time: 45 min

core_ingredients: Fresh fish, onion, tomato, cooking oil, salt
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger, coriander
Method: Stew
Steps: Prepare the Fish: Make deep, shallow slits on the sides of the fish. Mix the garlic-ginger paste, lemon juice, turmeric, chili powder, oil, and salt in a bowl. Rub this marinade thoroughly over the fish, including inside the slits. Let it marinate for at least 30 minutes to 1 hour.
Grill the Fish: Preheat your charcoal grill or oven to high heat. Grease the grill grates or a baking tray with oil. Grill the fish for 15-20 minutes, flipping once, until fully cooked and slightly charred.
Prepare the Coconut Sauce: In a pot, fry the onions in a little oil until soft. Add the garlic-ginger paste, turmeric, curry powder, and chilies, cooking for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the thick coconut milk and tamarind paste. Simmer over low heat until the sauce thickens. Season with salt and add lemon juice to taste.
Combine and Serve: Dip the grilled fish into the sauce, covering it thoroughly. For the best "kupaka" flavor, place the coated fish back on the grill for 2–3 minutes to char the sauce onto the fish, or simply pour the remaining sauce over the fish before serving.
Time: 40 min

core_ingredients: Small dried fish, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, black pepper
Method: Fry/Stew
Steps: Wash the dagaa thoroughly 2-3 times in hot water to remove sand and reduce bitterness. Drain well.
Fry Dagaa: Heat oil in a pan and fry the cleaned, dried dagaa until they are golden brown and crunchy.
Sauté Aromatics: In the same pan, add onions and fry until soft. Add garlic, chopped tomatoes, and tomato paste, cooking until the tomatoes are mushy.
Stew the Dagaa: Add the fried dagaa back into the pot. Add spices (curry powder) and salt to taste.
Simmer: Add a little water or coconut milk for gravy, cover, and simmer on low heat for 5-10 minutes.
Finish: Remove from heat, squeeze lemon juice over the top, and garnish with coriande
Time: 20 min

core_ingredients: Cooking bananas, beef, onion, tomato, oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger
Method: Stew
Steps: Brown the Meat: Heat oil in a large pot, sauté onions and garlic until soft. Add the beef and cook until browned on all sides.
Make the Stew Base: Add chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, and spices (turmeric/curry, salt, pepper). Cook, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes are mashed and form a thick sauce.
Cook the Meat: Add water or broth to cover the meat, cover the pot, and simmer until the meat is nearly tender (about 20-30 minutes).
Add Bananas: Add the peeled green bananas, ensuring they are submerged in the broth. Cover and cook for about 10–15 minutes, or until the bananas are softened.
Add Coconut Milk: Pour in the coconut milk, stirring gently, and simmer for another 5-10 minutes over low heat to combine the flavors.
Serve: Garnish with coriander/cilantro (optional) and serve hot
Time: 45 min

core_ingredients: Beef, cooking bananas, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger
Method: Stew
Steps: Prepare the Meat: Clean and dry the beef, cutting it into uniform, bite-sized pieces.
Marinate: In a large bowl, combine yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, spices, food coloring, and salt. Mix well. Add the beef, ensuring every piece is coated.
Rest: Cover and marinate in the refrigerator for at least 2 hours, or preferably overnight for the best flavor.
Cook/Grill:
Charcoal Grill (Preferred): Skewer the beef and grill over medium coals, turning frequently until charred outside and tender inside.
Oven: Preheat to 200°C (400°F) and grill on an oiled tray until cooked.
Pan-Fry: Use a grill pan on high heat with a little oil to sear the beef until tender and smoky.
Finish: If the meat is too tough, you can steam it for 10-15 minutes with a little water before the final sear.
Serve: Serve hot, often accompanied by chips (fries), kachumbari (onion-tomato salad), and lemon wedges.
Time: 45 min

Spices: Garlic
Method: Fry
Prep the Leaves: If using fresh leaves, pluck them from the stalks, wash thoroughly, and pound them in a mortar (kinu) to a fine texture.
Initial Boiling: Place the pounded leaves in a saucepan with water (covering them) and salt. Boil for 30–60 minutes until tender. Note: Ensure the leaves are well-boiled to remove any toxicity.
Add Ingredients: Add the chopped onion, garlic, and tomatoes. Add cooking oil and let it simmer until the vegetables are soft.
Coconut & Peanut Butter: Stir in the coconut cream and peanut butter. If the mixture is too dry, add a little water or more coconut milk.
Final Simmer: Reduce heat and cook for another 10–15 minutes, allowing the sauce to thicken and flavors to merge.
Serve: Serve hot with rice, ugali, or chapati
Time: 15 min

core_ingredients: Amaranth leaves, onion, tomato, cooking oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic
Steps: Sauté Aromatics: Heat oil in a large pot (sufuria). Add chopped onions and cook until soft and translucent.
Add Tomatoes & Spices: Add the tomatoes, curry powder, and garlic/chilies if using. Cook for 3-5 minutes until the tomatoes form a thick sauce.
Cook the Greens: Add the chopped spinach/mchicha to the pot. Stir well and let it wilt down, which takes about 3-5 minutes.
Add Cream/Peanut Butter: Pour in the coconut milk and stir in the peanut butter. Mix thoroughly until combined.
Simmer: Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, until the sauce is thick and creamy.
Serve: Season with salt to taste and serve hot with ugali or rice.
Time: 15 min

core_ingredients: Mixed leafy greens, onion, tomato, oil
recipes:
Method: Fry
Steps: Sauté Aromatics: Heat the cooking oil in a large saucepan. Add the chopped onions and garlic. Cook over medium heat until they are soft and translucent (about 2–3 minutes).
Add Tomatoes: Add the tomatoes and optional spices (turmeric/curry powder). Cook until the tomatoes are soft and turn into a paste.
Wilt the Greens: Add the chopped leafy greens. Note: They will look like a lot, but they will drastically reduce in size. Stir well to mix with the tomato mixture, then cover the pot and let them steam for 2–3 minutes.
Add Coconut Cream: Pour in the coconut cream. Stir well to combine.
Simmer: Reduce the heat to low and let the greens simmer for another 3–5 minutes until the sauce thickens and the greens are fully cooked but still green.
Serve: Remove from heat and serve immediately.
Time: 15 min

Steps: Boil Potatoes: Place potatoes in a pot, cover with water, and add a pinch of salt. Boil until tender but still firm (avoid overcooking, as they will fall apart when fried).
Cool & Slice: Drain the potatoes and let them cool completely. Cut them into thick slices or halves.
Make Batter: In a bowl, mix the gram flour, turmeric powder, garlic-ginger paste, and salt. Gradually add water while stirring until you achieve a thick, smooth consistency that coats the back of a spoon.
Heat Oil: Heat oil in a deep pan or wok (karai) over medium-high heat.
Dip & Fry: Dip each potato slice into the batter, ensuring it is well coated. Carefully place into the hot oil.
Fry: Fry for 3-4 minutes until golden brown and crispy.
Serve: Remove with a slotted spoon, drain on paper towels, and serve hot with tea or chutney.
Time: 25–35 min

Method: Boil
Steps: Prepare the Cassava: Peel the outer skin of the cassava, wash thoroughly to remove dirt, and cut into pieces (usually 2-3 inch/chunks). Remove the woody core fiber if necessary.
Boil: Place the cassava in a large pot (sufuria). Add enough water to completely cover the cassava pieces. Add the salt.
Cook: Bring to a boil over medium heat. Cover the pot and let it cook for 15-20 minutes until the cassava is soft and tender when pierced with a fork.
Drain & Serve: Drain all the excess water. Serve the cassava hot, ideally with a squeeze of fresh lemon and raw chili, or alongside coffee/tea.
Time: 25–35 min

core_ingredients: Wheat flour, water, cooking oil, salt
recipes:
Steps:Prepare the Dough: Mix flour, salt, and sugar in a large bowl. Gradually add warm water, mixing until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms.
Knead: Knead for 5–10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Rest (1st Time): Rub a little oil over the dough, cover with a damp cloth or plastic wrap, and rest for 30-60 minutes.
Create Layers (Crucial): Divide the dough into small balls. Roll a ball out, brush with oil/ghee, roll it into a cylinder ("sausage" shape), then coil it like a snail. Repeat for all.
Rest (2nd Time): Let the coils rest for 15-20 minutes.
Roll: Roll each coil into a thin circular flatbread.
Cook: Heat a skillet/pan over medium heat. Place a chapati in the dry pan, cooking for 30 seconds until bubbles form. Flip, brush with oil, and cook the other side, turning until both sides are golden brown.
Serve: Serve warm with stew, tea, or vegetables. 
Time: 30–40 min

core_ingredients: Chicken, onion, tomato, cooking oil, salt
recipes:
Steps:Boil the Chicken: In a large pot (sufuria), add the chicken pieces, salt, and enough water to cover them. Boil over medium heat for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until the meat is very tender. Note: Do not discard the remaining broth.
Sauté Aromatics: In a separate large pot, heat oil and sauté the onions until golden brown. Add the ginger-garlic paste and bell pepper, stirring for 1-2 minutes until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes & Spices: Add the tomatoes, tomato paste, curry powder/masala, and black pepper. Cook until the tomatoes are soft and form a thick paste.
Combine: Add the boiled chicken pieces to the tomato mixture. Stir well to coat the chicken and let it fry for about 5 minutes.
Simmer: Pour in the reserved chicken broth (and extra water if needed for more gravy). Stir in the dissolved Royco. Cover and simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander leaves, stir, and turn off the heat.
Serve: Serve hot with Tanzanian staples like ugali, chapati, or rice. Time: 30 min

Method: Boil
Steps:Prepare the Paste: In a bowl, mix the flour with 1/2 cup of cold water. Stir until smooth with no lumps.
Boil Water: Bring the remaining 3-4 cups of water to a boil in a large saucepan.
Combine: Slowly pour the flour mixture into the boiling water while stirring constantly with a wooden spoon to prevent lumping.
Thicken: Continue to stir on medium heat until the porridge thickens and starts to bubble (about 5-10 minutes).
Simmer & Customize: Reduce heat. If you like it thinner, add more water. Stir in sugar to taste. For a richer taste, add milk.
Add Sourness (Optional): Stir in lemon juice or vinegar for a fermented taste.
Finish: Simmer for another 5 minutes.
Serve: Serve hot in a bowl or mug
Time: 10 min

core_ingredients: Chickpea flour, onion, garlic, oil, spices
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger, berbere
Method: Stew
Steps: Sauté Aromatics: Heat the oil or niter kibbeh in a medium-sized pot over medium heat. Add the diced onions and cook until they are soft and translucent, but not browned, about 5-8 minutes.
Add Spices and Tomato: Add the minced garlic and berbere spice to the onions. Sauté for another 1-2 minutes until aromatic. Add the tomato paste and mix well, cooking for 30 seconds.
Prepare the Shiro Base: Gradually whisk in the shiro powder to the onion mixture to avoid lumps.
Simmer the Stew: Slowly add the hot water while stirring constantly. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover, and let it simmer for 20 to 30 minutes.
Finalize Consistency: Stir frequently to ensure it does not stick to the bottom. If it is too thick, add a little more hot water until it reaches a thick gravy consistency.
Finish and Serve: The shiro is ready when the oil floats on top. Add salt to taste and garnish with sliced jalapeno if desired.
Time: 30–40 min

core_ingredients: Red lentils, onion, garlic, oil, berbere
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger, berbere
Method: Stew
Steps: Sauté Aromatics: Melt the niter kibbeh (or heat oil) in a pot over medium heat. Add the finely diced onions and cook until soft and golden (about 10-15 minutes).
Add Spices: Add garlic, ginger, and berbere. Sauté for 1–2 minutes, adding a splash of water if needed to prevent sticking.
Combine Ingredients: Stir in the tomato paste and cooked lentils, coating them well with the spice mixture.
Simmer: Add 2.5–3 cups of water/broth and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 20-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils are tender and stew-like.
Finish: Add salt to taste. For an authentic touch, add 1/2 tsp of mekelesha (garam masala blend) and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Serve: Serve hot with Injera.
Time: 30–40 min

core_ingredients: Yellow split peas, onion, garlic, oil
recipes:
Spices: Garlic, ginger, berbere
Method: Stew
Steps: Prep the Peas: Rinse the split peas thoroughly, then soak them in warm water for at least 30–60 minutes to reduce cooking time. Drain before using.
Sauté Aromatics: In a pot, heat the oil or niter kibbeh over medium heat. Add the chopped onions and sauté for 5–7 minutes until translucent and soft.
Add Spices: Add the garlic, ginger, turmeric, and Serrano/jalapeño. Sauté for another 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Cook the Stew: Add the soaked, drained split peas and the 3 cups of water/stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to low heat, cover with a lid, and let it simmer for 45–60 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Finish: Add salt to taste. The peas should be soft and tender (add a splash of water if it becomes too dry). For a creamier texture, mash some of the peas with a spoon.
Serve: Serve warm with Injera flatbread.
Time: 30–40 min

Spices: Garlic, ginger, berbere (optional black pepper)
Method: Fry
Steps:Prep: Slice meat, onions, peppers, and garlic. Season beef with salt and a little berbere.
Sear Meat: Heat a wok or large pan to high heat. Add a little oil, then sear the meat in batches until browned on the outside but still tender inside. Remove meat and set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: Add the niter kebbeh to the same pan. Stir-fry onions until translucent (about 5 mins). Add garlic, ginger, and berbere, cooking for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Combine: Return the meat to the pan. Add jalapeños, tomatoes, and red wine if using.
Finish: Stir-fry for another 2–3 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the meat is cooked to your liking. Top with fresh rosemary.
Serve: Serve immediately with injera, a popular Ethiopian bread made from teff flour.
Time: 25–30 min

Spices: Garlic, ginger, berbere
Method: Stew
Steps: Prepare the Chicken: Cut chicken into pieces, wash with lemon juice, and set aside.
Caramelize Onions (Low & Slow): Place minced onions in a dry, heavy-bottomed pot (or with very little oil) and cook over low heat for 30–60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden and reduced.
Add Butter and Spices: Add the Niter Kibbeh and sauté the onions for another 10–15 minutes. Stir in the berbere, garlic, ginger, and tomato paste, cooking for 5–10 minutes to form a fragrant paste.
Cook the Stew: Add chicken, water/broth (enough to cover the chicken), and salt. Cover and simmer on low for 45–60 minutes until the chicken is very tender.
Add Eggs: Add the hard-boiled eggs, ensuring they are submerged in the sauce, and simmer for another 10-15 minutes.
