Slow Cook: Place the chopped fat in a large pot on the stove over low heat. Alternatively, put it in a slow cooker on low.
Render: Allow the fat to melt slowly, stirring occasionally to prevent scorching. The liquid will turn clear, and the solid bits (cracklings) will sink and turn golden brown, taking roughly 1–2 hours.
Strain: Once all fat is liquid, strain it through several layers of cheesecloth into a clean glass jar or container to remove solid impurities.
Store: Allow the liquid to cool. It will turn solid and opaque white/off-white.

Prepare the Maize Meal: Pour the 1 cup of hot water over the maize meal in a bowl, stir to mix, and set aside to cool slightly.
Combine Dry Ingredients: In a large mixing bowl, combine the cake flour, yeast, salt, and sugar.
Mix Dough: Add the cooled maize meal mixture to the dry ingredients. Add the lukewarm water gradually, mixing by hand until a dough forms.
Knead: Turn the dough onto a floured surface and knead for about 10 minutes until smooth, adding extra flour if the dough is too sticky.
Rise: Place the dough in a greased bowl, cover with a cloth, and let it rise in a warm place until it has doubled in size.
Shape & Second Rise: Knead the dough again briefly to remove air bubbles. Shape into a loaf and place on a baking tray or in a greased loaf pan. Allow it to rise again for 30 minutes.
Bake: Preheat your oven to 180°C. Score the top of the bread with a sharp knife and bake for 30–40 minutes until golden brown.

core_ingredients: Dried black-eyed peas (500g), palm oil (3 tbsp)
recipes:
Prep the Beans: Soak the beans for at least 1 hour or overnight. Rub the beans together to remove the skins, then wash repeatedly until the skin is gone and the water runs clear.
Blend: Blend the peeled beans with the habanero peppers and onions, adding water sparingly until a slightly coarse, thick paste is formed.
Mix & Season: Transfer the paste to a large bowl. Add salt, bouillon cubes, and the warmed palm oil. Whisk the mixture vigorously to incorporate air for a fluffy texture.
Add Greens: Fold in the spinach or cocoyam leaves, along with a bit more water if needed, ensuring the mixture is well combined.
Wrap: Warm the banana leaves over an open flame to make them pliable. Shape the leaves into a container inside a small bowl, pour in the mixture, and seal tightly. Use aluminum foil as an outer layer to prevent leaks.
Steam: Place the wrapped packages into a large pot lined with leaves or a steamer basket. Add boiling water to the pot (avoid flooding the packages) and cover tightly.
Cook: Steam for approximately 1.5 to 2 hours, checking every 15–20 minutes to add more hot water to the pot, ensuring it doesn’t dry out.
Serve: Let the koki cool for a few minutes to solidify before serving.

Prepare the Beans (Optional Soak): Pick through the beans to remove stones or debris. For quicker cooking, soak the black-eyed peas overnight. If you are in a rush, you can skip this, but soaking makes them cook faster.
Boil the Leaves (Color Extraction): Wash the dried sorghum leaves thoroughly to remove sand. In a large saucepan, add the leaves and about 4 cups of water. Bring to a boil and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes until the water becomes a deep red-brown color.
Cook the Beans: Remove the leaves from the water (optional, some leave them in) or keep them in for a deeper color. Add the rinsed beans to the pot. Add the baking soda (if using, this helps soften the beans and enhance the color).
Tenderize the Beans: Cook the beans for 30–45 minutes on medium heat until they are soft (about 80% cooked). If they become too dry, add a little hot water.
Add the Rice: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear. Add the rice to the pot with the beans and beans-cooking liquid.
Season and Combine: Add salt to taste. Add the oil if desired. Ensure there is enough water to cook the rice (the water level should be about 1 inch above the rice/beans mixture).
Simmer and Steam: Stir briefly, cover the pot tightly (use foil or a clean plastic bag under the lid to seal in steam), and cook on low heat for 15-20 minutes, or until the rice is fully cooked and the liquid is absorbed.
Finalize: Remove the sorghum leaves if they are still in the pot. Fluff with a fork and serve.

Prepare the Mixture: In a large bowl, mix the fermented corn dough and cassava dough with water until smooth, ensuring no lumps.
Combine and Season: Pour the mixture into a sturdy pot. Add salt. Mix well, ensuring the consistency is like thin porridge.
Cook and Stir (Driving): Place the pot on medium heat and stir continuously with a wooden spoon to prevent lumps.
Thicken: As the mixture cooks, it will thicken into a heavy dough. Keep stirring vigorously for about 5-10 minutes until it forms a solid mass.
Steam the Banku: Poke a few holes into the dough, add about 1/4 cup of water to the pot (or enough to cover the bottom without submerging the dough), cover the pot tightly, and turn the heat to medium-low.
Final Cooking: Let it steam for 30–40 minutes, until the dough turns a slightly lighter, yellow-white color.
Final Knead and Shape: Knead the banku together until it is smooth. Scoop portions into clear film/plastic, a bowl, or leaves, and mold into balls

core_ingredients: Egusi seeds (200g), greens (300g), palm oil (3 tbsp)
recipes:
Prep: Grind the egusi seeds, then mix with a little water/stock to form a thick paste. Parboil your meat and stockfish with onions and seasoning until tender.
Fry Base: Heat the palm oil in a pot, add sliced onions and the ground egusi paste. Fry on low-medium heat for 10–15 minutes, stirring frequently so it doesn't burn.
Combine: Pour in your meat/fish stock. Stir in ground crayfish, pepper, and seasoning cubes. Cover and let it simmer for 15–20 minutes until the oil separates and rises to the top.
Add Veggies & Protein: Add the cooked meat, smoked fish, and leafy greens (ugue/spinach).
Finish: Stir, cover the pot, and cook for another 5–10 minutes until the leaves are wilted. Serve hot with pounded yam, eba, or rice.

Prepare the Meat: Slice the beef very thinly against the grain (approx. 1/8 inch). Pat completely dry with paper towels to ensure it stays tender.
Make the Marinade: In a large bowl, mix the ground peanuts, cayenne, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, ginger, bouillon cubes, and salt. Add oil to create a thick paste.
Marinate: Add the beef to the spice mix, coating each piece thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight for best flavor.
Skewer: Thread the meat onto soaked skewers, ensuring the pieces are packed together to prevent drying out.
Cook:
Grill: Preheat grill to high heat (450°F+). Grill for 3-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Oven/Broiler: Preheat oven to 450°F. Bake for 10 minutes, then turn broiler to high for 2-3 minutes to get a smoky, charred texture.
Serve: Sprinkle with extra yaji spice. Serve hot with sliced raw red onions, tomatoes, and cabbage

Peel and Clean Beans: Soak the beans for 10-15 minutes, then rub them between your hands to remove the skins. Rinse multiple times to separate and remove all skins.
Blend: Blend the peeled beans along with the onions, red bell pepper, and scotch bonnets. Add water slowly to create a smooth, thick consistency.
Season the Mixture: Pour the batter into a large bowl. Add vegetable oil, seasoning cubes, crayfish, and salt to taste. Stir well, adding more water if the mixture is too thick.
Add Fillings: Mix in your preferred proteins (cooked fish, hard-boiled eggs, etc.).
Package and Steam: Pour the mixture into container (ramekins, foil containers) or traditional leaves (Uma leaves). Place them in a pot with about 1 inch of boiling water, cover tightly, and steam for 45-60 minutes, adding more water to the pot if necessary.
Check & Serve: When firm, remove from the pot and let it cool slightly.

Prep Meats: Boil meat (beef, shaki, pomo) with seasoning and onions until tender. Keep the stock low.
Prepare Okra: Chop half of the okra into tiny pieces and blend the other half (using minimal water).
Fry Base: In a clean pot, heat palm oil on medium heat. Add locust beans and pepper mix, frying for 3-5 minutes.
Combine: Add the cooked meats and smoked fish into the pot. Add the meat stock, crayfish, and seasoning. Allow to simmer for 5 minutes.
Add Okra: Add the blended and chopped okra. Stir thoroughly and cook for 5–7 minutes. Do not overcook to maintain green color and crunch.
Add Greens: Add chopped spinach or pumpkin leaves (if using), simmer for 1-2 minutes, then remove from heat.
Serve: Serve with swallows like Eba, Pounded Yam, or Amala.

Prep the Pepper: Roughly blend green bell peppers, scotch bonnets, and one onion without water (or strain if needed).
Bleach Palm Oil: Pour palm oil into a clean pot, cover, and heat on medium-low for 10-15 minutes until it turns light yellow/translucent. Warning: Do this in a ventilated kitchen. Let it cool before opening.
Fry Aromatics: Reheat the bleached oil. Add chopped onions and locust beans (iru), frying for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Cook the Sauce: Add the blended pepper mixture. Fry on medium heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent burning, until the sauce reduces and oil separates on top.
Add Protein: Add the cooked assorted meat, stockfish, ground crayfish, bouillon cubes, and salt. Add a little beef stock if too thick.
Simmer: Add boiled eggs, cover the pot, and let it simmer on low heat for another 10-15 minutes.
Serve: Best served with Ofada rice, wrapped in leaves (Uma) or regular white rice.

Prep and Boil Meat: Boil assorted meats and stockfish with onions, seasoning, and salt until tender. Save the stock.
Prepare Vegetables: Blanch spinach in hot water for 60 seconds, then immediately immerse in cold water to stop cooking. Squeeze out all excess water thoroughly.
Fry Base: Heat palm oil in a pot. Add chopped onions and locust beans (iru), frying for 2 minutes.
Cook Pepper Mix: Pour in the blended pepper mix and fry for 10-15 minutes until the oil separates and rises to the top.
Combine: Add the crayfish, stockfish, smoked fish, and boiled meat. Add a little stock and simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Add Greens: Stir in the dried spinach. Let it simmer uncovered for 3-5 minutes to avoid a soggy texture.
Finish: Taste for salt and seasoning, then remove from heat.

Prepare Meat: Wash the meat thoroughly and place in a large pot. Add chopped onions, seasoning cubes, salt, and half of the blended peppers.
Cook Meat: Steam the meat for about 10-15 minutes until it releases its juices.
Add Liquid & Spices: Add water to cover the meat, then add the Pepper Soup Spice, ground Ehu, crayfish, and remaining peppers.
Simmer: Cover the pot and cook on medium heat for 30-50 minutes, or until the meat is tender.
Finish: Add the chopped scent leaves (or Uziza) and simmer for an additional 5 minutes.
Serve: Adjust salt/pepper and serve hot on its own or with boiled yam, plantain, or white rice

Prep the Abacha: Soak dry abacha in hot water for 3–5 minutes to soften. Drain thoroughly and set aside.
Make the Dressing: In a bowl or pot, combine palm oil and potash water. Mix thoroughly until the oil turns into a thick, bright yellow paste.
Add Spices: Add ground crayfish, pepper, ground ehu, ogiri, diced onions, and salt/seasoning cubes to the yellow paste and stir well.
Mix Everything: Add the soaked, drained abacha, ugba (oil bean), and kanda (kpomo) to the paste. Mix well until every strand of cassava is coated.
Garnish: Add sliced utazi leaves and mix. Garnish with more onions, garden eggs, and fried fish.
Serving: Serve warm or at room temperature. It does not need to be cooked on a stove.

Prep the Beans: Soak beans for 1–2 hours, then peel off the skin by rubbing between your hands or using a food processor. Rinse thoroughly until the skin is gone.
Blend: Blend the peeled beans, onion, and peppers into a very thick paste, adding only tiny amounts of water as needed. The texture should be smooth and thick, not runny.
Whisk/Fluff: Pour the paste into a bowl and whisk vigorously for 5–10 minutes, or use a blender/mixer to whip it. This makes the batter light, fluffy, and prevents it from sinking.
Season: Stir in the salt and bouillon cubes. Add chopped onion (optional).
Fry: Heat oil to 350-375°F (175-190°C). Scoop the batter with a spoon and drop into the hot oil in batches.
Drain: Fry for 3–5 minutes until golden brown on both sides. Transfer to a paper towel-lined plate or a colander to drain excess oil.

Prepare the Pepper Base: Blend the red bell peppers, tomatoes, onions, and scotch bonnets until smooth. Pour into a pot and boil for 10-12 minutes to reduce the moisture.
Fry the Stew: Heat vegetable oil in a large pot, add sliced onions, and fry for 2-3 minutes. Add tomato paste and fry for another 2-3 minutes. Pour in the boiled pepper mix, curry powder, thyme, and bay leaves. Fry on medium heat until the oil separates from the pepper sauce (about 10 minutes).
Add Stock and Rice: Add the chicken/beef stock and bring to a boil. Stir in the washed parboiled rice. Ensure the liquid level is just at the same level as the rice (not too high to prevent sogginess).
Steam: Cover the pot with aluminum foil to trap steam, then cover with a tight-fitting lid. Reduce the heat to the lowest setting.
Cook: Cook for 30–40 minutes. Stir half-way through (after 20 mins) to ensure even cooking.
spices_seasoning: Garlic, thyme, bay leaf, salt, chili

recipes:
Marinate the Chicken: In a large bowl, combine onions, garlic, lemon juice, mustard, oil, chili, vinegar, salt, and pepper. Add the chicken, coating it well. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or ideally overnight.
Sear the Chicken: Remove the chicken from the marinade, shaking off excess onions (save the onions and marinade). In a large pot or Dutch oven, brown the chicken over medium-high heat on both sides (about 5–6 minutes per side). Set the chicken aside on a plate.
Caramelize the Onions: Add the reserved onions and marinade to the same pot. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the onions are soft and deeply caramelized (10-15 minutes).
Braise: Return the chicken to the pot, covering it with the onions. Add the chicken stock, bouillon cube, and bay leaves.
Simmer: Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 30-40 minutes until the chicken is tender and cooked through (165°F / 75°C internal temp).
Finish: Stir in the green olives (if using) and simmer uncovered for another 5 minutes.
Serve: Remove the bay leaves and serve hot over rice

Brown the Meat: Heat peanut oil in a large pot. Season the meat with salt and brown it on all sides over medium heat. Add diced onions and garlic, cooking until softened.
Add Tomatoes & Veggies: Stir in the tomato paste and cook for 2-3 minutes to reduce acidity. Add chopped sweet potatoes, carrots, cayenne pepper, curry powder, and bouillon.
Add Peanut Butter: Whisk the peanut butter with 1 cup of hot water or broth to create a smooth paste before adding to the pot (this prevents clumping). Add the remaining broth.
Simmer: Bring the stew to a boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer for 30–60 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, until the meat is tender and a thin film of oil separates on the surface.
Finish & Serve: Adjust seasoning and consistency. Serve hot over white rice or fufu.

Brown Meat: Heat oil in a large pot and brown the seasoned meat on all sides. Remove meat and set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: Add diced onions, garlic, and tomato paste to the pot. Sauté until translucent, scraping up browned bits.
Simmer Stew: Add broth, browned meat, carrots, and sweet potatoes. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 45-60 minutes.
Add Peanut Butter: Whisk in the peanut butter and simmer for another 10–15 minutes until the stew thickens and oil rises to the top.
Finish & Serve: Stir in vegetables (like cabbage) and chili, simmering until tender. Serve hot over white rice

Prepare the Salt Water Solution: In a small bowl, mix 1/4 cup of water with 1–2 teaspoons of salt until dissolved. Set aside.
Peel the Plantains: Cut off both ends of the plantains. Make a shallow slit along the natural ridges of the skin from end to end. Carefully remove the peel with your fingers.
Slice the Plantains: Using a mandoline or a sharp knife, slice the plantains into very thin, uniform rounds (about 1/8 inch or thinner).
Heat the Oil: Fill a deep pan with 2–3 inches of oil and heat it over medium-high heat until it reaches roughly 350-375°F (175–190°C).
Fry the Chips:
Method A (Direct to Oil): Slice the plantain directly into the hot oil using a mandoline, taking care not to burn your hands.
Method B (Pre-sliced): Carefully drop slices into the oil, one by one, to prevent sticking.
Do not overcrowd the pan; fry in batches..
Add Salt Water & Finish: After about 2 minutes of frying, add 1–2 tablespoons of the salt-water solution directly into the hot oil. It will sizzle intensely. Stir gently to ensure the salt coats the chips.
Remove and Drain: Continue frying until the plantain slices are golden brown and sound stiff when moved with a spoon (about 5–8 minutes total). Remove with a slotted spoon and place on paper towels to drain excess oil.
Cool: Let the chips cool completely to become fully crunchy. They can be stored in an airtight container for 2–3 weeks.

Prep and Steam Meat: Blend 1 onion, ginger, and garlic. In a large pot, mix the meats with this blend, salt, and seasoning. Steam on medium heat for 10-15 minutes until it releases its own juices.
Add Vegetables: Add tomatoes, remaining onions, and habanero peppers to the meat pot. Add a small amount of water if necessary and simmer until the vegetables are soft (about 10 mins).
Blend Veggies: Remove the soft onion, tomato, and pepper from the pot, blend them, and pour back into the pot.
Add Palm Cream: Dilute the palm nut concentrate with 2-3 cups of hot water, mix well, and pour into the pot.
Simmer and Add Flavor: Add the prekeses and smoked fish. Stir well to combine. Reduce heat to low-medium, allowing it to simmer for 20-30 minutes until the soup thickens and palm oil floats to the top.
Final Adjustments: Taste and add more salt, pepper, or shrimp powder if needed. Remove prekese before serving.
Serve: Serve hot with Fufu, Rice Balls, or Yam.

Prep the Yam: Peel the brown skin off the yam. Cut the yam into thick slices (roughly 1/2 inch to 1 inch thick). Cut those slices into smaller, uniform rectangular chips or rounds.
Wash: Wash the slices thoroughly in clean water 2-3 times to remove excess starch, which prevents sticking.
Season: Place the yam in a bowl and add water to cover. Add salt and optional turmeric powder, letting it sit for 10-15 minutes.
Heat Oil: Heat enough oil in a deep fryer or large pan until it reaches high heat.
Fry: Carefully place the yam slices into the hot oil. Fry on medium-high heat for about 7-10 minutes, flipping occasionally until golden brown and crispy.
Drain: Remove the yams and place them on a paper towel to absorb excess oil.

Mix Dry Ingredients: In a large bowl, sift together the flour, sugar, salt, and ground nutmeg. Add the instant yeast and mix thoroughly.
Add Warm Water: Gradually add the warm water (not hot, or it will kill the yeast) to the dry mixture. Mix by hand or with a sturdy spoon until combined.
Beat the Batter: This is the key to fluffy Gbofloto. Use your hand to beat the batter for 2–5 minutes. The texture should be smooth, thick, and stretchy—thicker than pancake batter but not as firm as bread dough.
Rise (Proof): Cover the bowl tightly with plastic wrap or a kitchen towel. Place it in a warm, draft-free place for 1 to 2 hours, or until the batter has doubled in size and looks very bubbly.
Heat Oil: Heat at least 3 inches of oil in a deep pot over medium heat. To test if it is ready, drop a tiny bit of batter; it should rise to the surface immediately, but not burn instantly.
Fry the Gbofloto:
Deflate the risen batter by mixing it briefly.
Scoop some batter in your hand, close your fist, and squeeze the batter out between your thumb and index finger into the hot oil.
Do not overcrowd the pot; fry in batches.
Golden Brown: As they rise to the top, use a slotted spoon to turn them continuously to ensure an even golden-brown color on all sides.
Drain: Remove from the oil and place on paper towels to absorb excess oil.

Prepare the Base: If using fresh palm nuts, boil them for 15-20 minutes until tender, then pound in a mortar to separate the pulp from the nuts. Add hot water to the pulp, strain, and collect the thick extract. If using canned concentrate, pour directly into the pot and mix with water to achieve a medium consistency.
Cook Meat & Extract: Boil the extracted palm cream on medium-high heat for 15-20 minutes, or until the oil starts rising to the surface. Add your pre-cooked assorted meat and stockfish to the pot.
Add Spices: Add the ground crayfish, ground banga spice, chopped onions, peppers, and the oburunbebe stick. Let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Add Seafood: Add the cleaned smoked fish and fresh catfish. Cover the pot and allow to cook for another 10-15 minutes until the fish is cooked through and the soup thickens.
Finishing: Add the washed beletete leaves (or bitter leaves), adjust seasoning with salt/cubes, and let it simmer for another 2-3 minutes.
Serve: Remove the oburunbebe stick and serve hot

Prepare the Fish: Wash the tilapia thoroughly with lemon or lime juice to remove the fishy smell. Pat dry, season with salt, ginger, and garlic powder, and let it marinate for 15-30 minutes.
Fry the Fish: Heat vegetable oil in a large pan and deep-fry the tilapia until golden brown and crispy. Remove and set aside.
Prepare the Stew Base: Using the same oil (reduce if necessary), sauté the sliced onions until translucent. Add the tomato paste, garlic, and ginger, cooking for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add Tomatoes & Simmer: Stir in the blended tomatoes, pepper, curry powder, and paprika. Cook on medium heat until the water evaporates and the oil rises to the top (about 10-15 minutes).
Combine and Cook: Add 1-2 cups of water or stock to achieve your desired consistency. Gently add the fried tilapia, cover, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes on low heat.
Finish: Garnish with fresh coriander or green onions. Serve hot

Prepare the Leaves: If using fresh leaves, remove stems, wash thoroughly, and pound until smooth in a mortar. Frozen leaves should be rinsed with cold water and squeezed dry.
Cook the Meat: Season the beef/goat with half the onion, ginger, garlic, and salt. Steam it in a pot without water until it releases its own juices, then add a little water and cook until tender.
Add Ingredients: Stir in the peanut butter and a little more water to create a sauce base. Add the pounded cassava leaves, smoked fish, and remaining onions.
Simmer: Cover and let the stew simmer on low to medium heat for 45–60 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
Add Oil: Add the palm oil and hot pepper. Simmer for an additional 10–15 minutes until the oil rises to the top.
Serve: Taste for salt and serve hot.

Prep the Beans: Pick, wash, and boil beans for 35-40 minutes until tender but not mushy.
Prep the Rice: Wash the rice multiple times until the water runs clear to remove starch.
Combine: Add the washed rice, sliced onions, salt, seasoning, and oil to the cooked beans.
Cook: Add water to about 1 inch above the rice level. Cover the pot tightly to steam, cooking on low-to-medium heat for 20-30 minutes until both are soft.
Serve: Fluff with a fork and serve with stew or fried plantain

Sauté Veggies: Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Add onions and peppers, cooking until softened (approx. 5–7 mins). Add garlic and cook for 1 more minute until fragrant.
Make Tomato Sauce: Add the chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, paprika, cumin, salt, and pepper. Stir well and let it simmer for 10–15 minutes until the tomatoes have reduced and thickened into a chunky sauce.
Add Eggs: Using a spoon, make 4–6 small wells in the sauce. Carefully crack an egg into each well.
Cook to Perfection: Cover the skillet and simmer on low heat for 5–10 minutes, or until the egg whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Serve: Garnish with fresh parsley or cilantro and a drizzle of olive oil. Serve immediately with fresh bread

Prepare the Sauce: In a large pot (or couscous maker), heat oil and butter. Brown the chicken, grated onions, salt, pepper, and cinnamon.
Simmer: Add chickpeas, water to cover, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to medium, cover, and let it simmer.
Steam the Noodles: While the sauce cooks, mix the rechta noodles with oil and steam them in the top part of a couscous maker for 10-15 minutes.
Add Vegetables: Add the turnips and zucchini to the stew and cook until all vegetables are tender (roughly another 20-30 minutes).
Second Steam/Assembly: Remove noodles from the steamer, break them up with butter, and optionally steam a second time for 10-15 minutes.
Serve: Place the noodles in a large dish, garnish with chicken, chickpeas, vegetables, and ladle the broth over the top.

Prep Beans: Soak dried beans in water overnight with a pinch of baking soda, then drain and rinse.
Sauté Meat & Aromatics: Heat 4 tbsp olive oil in a pot. Sear the meat until brown, then add the grated onion and garlic, cooking until translucent.
Add Spices & Tomatoes: Add tomato paste, pureed tomatoes, paprika, cumin, black pepper, and herbs. Cook for 5 minutes until fragrant.
Simmer: Add the beans and cover with hot water. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low. Cover and simmer for 1.5 to 2 hours until the beans are tender and the sauce is thick.
Serve: Serve hot, traditionally with drizzle of olive oil, a splash of vinegar, and crusty bread.

Mix & Rub: In a large bowl, mix the semolina, salt, baking powder, and sugar. Pour in the oil/butter. Use your fingertips to rub the oil into the semolina until it resembles coarse, sandy crumbs.
Form Dough: Gradually add warm water while mixing. Knead gently for a few minutes until you have a smooth, firm dough. If it is too dry, add a little more water.
Rest: Cover the dough with a clean cloth and let it rest for at least 10-20 minutes.
Shape: Divide the dough into 2-3 equal balls. On a surface lightly dusted with semolina, flatten each ball with your hand or a rolling pin into a circle about 1/4 inch) thick.
Cook: Heat a non-stick skillet or tajine over medium-high heat. Place the flattened dough on the hot pan and prick the top all over with a fork to prevent bubbling.
Brown: Cook for about 3-4 minutes on each side, turning clockwise to ensure even browning, until golden spots appear.
Serve: Cut into wedges while warm. Best served with olive oil, honey, or stew.

Sauté the Base: Heat oil in a pot (or Dutch oven) on medium heat. Add the onion and lamb (or chicken) and cook until the meat is browned.
Add Spices & Aromatics: Add the chopped coriander, mint, chickpeas, paprika, cinnamon, salt, and pepper. Mix well and let it simmer for 4–5 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add Tomatoes & Water: Add the pureed tomatoes and tomato paste. Add 1/2 cup of water and bring to a boil.
Simmer: Pour in 4-5 cups of water, cover the pot, and let it simmer for about 45 minutes to 1 hour, or until the meat is tender.
Add Frik: Add the rinsed frik to the pot. Stir well. Let the soup simmer, uncovered, for another 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, until the frik is soft and the soup has thickened.
Serve: Garnish with fresh chopped coriander and serve hot with lemon wedges

Warm the Beans: Drain the fava beans and place them in a pot with 1/4 to 1/2 cup of water. Heat over medium heat.
Mash and Season: Mash the beans using a potato masher or fork, leaving some texture. Add the cumin, salt, and 2 tbsp olive oil.
Add Flavor: In a bowl, mix minced garlic with the lemon juice. Pour this sauce over the beans, stirring well to combine. Let it simmer for 5–10 minutes until creamy.
Serve and Garnish: Transfer to a bowl. Top generously with diced tomatoes, chopped parsley, sliced onions, and a liberal drizzle of high-quality olive oil.
Serve immediately with warm pita bread.

Crispy Onions: Toss onion slices in flour. Fry in oil over medium-high heat, stirring until deeply browned and crispy (15–20 minutes). Drain on a paper towel-lined plate.
Lentils: Boil lentils with cumin and garlic until tender (20–30 minutes).
Rice: Sauté vermicelli in the leftover onion oil, add washed rice, salt, and water. Cook until tender.
Pasta: Boil pasta until al dente, drain, and toss with a little oil to prevent sticking.
Tomato Sauce: Sauté minced garlic in oil, add coriander and chili flakes. Stir in tomato sauce, vinegar, and salt; simmer for 15 minutes until thickened.
Vinegar Sauce (Da'ah): Mix 1/4 cup white vinegar, 1/2 cup water, minced garlic, and cumin (optional, but traditional).
Assembly: Layer rice, then lentils, then pasta. Top generously with chickpeas, crispy onions, and tomato sauce. Serve the vinegar sauce on the side

Soak the Beans: Rinse the fava beans thoroughly and soak them in plenty of water for 12–24 hours. Change the water once or twice to ensure they soften properly.
Drain and Prep: Drain the beans completely and pat them dry. Important: Using water-logged beans will make the mixture fall apart.
Blend: In a food processor, combine the soaked beans, onion, garlic, parsley, cilantro, dill, and leeks. Blend until a smooth, almost paste-like consistency is reached.
Aerate: Continue blending until the mixture becomes light and fluffy.
Season: Add the coriander, cumin, salt, and pepper. Mix well. Tip: Only season the mixture right before frying to avoid darkening.
Shape: Mix in the baking soda. Shape the mixture into small, flat patties (walnut-sized). Press one side of the patty into a plate of sesame seeds.
Fry: Heat oil to 175 degrees celcius. Fry the patties in batches for 3-4 minutes until golden brown and crispy on the outside.
Serve: Drain on paper towels and serve immediately inside Egyptian pita bread with tahini sauce, salad, and pickles.

Prepare the Broth: Bring your chicken broth to a gentle simmer in a pot. Add salt/pepper and a bouillon cube if desired.
Add Molokhia: Add the frozen molokhia directly to the broth. Stir occasionally until fully incorporated and thawed, about 5–10 minutes over low-medium heat. Do not let it come to a rolling boil or the leaves will separate from the liquid.
Prepare the Tasha (Garlic Topping): In a small pan, melt the ghee over medium heat. Add the minced garlic and sauté until fragrant and lightly golden (about 2-3 minutes). Add the ground coriander and stir constantly for another minute until fragrant, being careful not to burn it.
Combine: Pour the hot tasha (garlic-coriander mixture) into the molokhia pot, making that iconic sizzling sound, and stir well.
Finish: Simmer for just 1–2 more minutes, then remove from heat. Leave the pot uncovered to keep the color vibrant.
Serve: Serve hot with white Egyptian rice, roasted chicken, and a side of pickled tomatoes or onions.

Activate Yeast: Mix warm water, sugar, and yeast. Let it stand for 5-10 minutes until foamy.
Make the Dough: In a large bowl, combine flour and salt. Add the yeast mixture and knead (by hand or with a mixer) for 10-15 minutes until the dough is soft and slightly tacky.
Rise: Cover the dough with a damp cloth or plastic wrap and set in a warm place for 1–2 hours, or until doubled in size.
Shape: Divide the dough into 8-10 equal balls. Cover them and let them rest for 15-20 minutes.
Roll and Coat: Dip each ball into the wheat bran, then roll it into a circle roughly 1/8 to 1/4 inch thick (about 5-6 inches wide). Let the flattened rounds rest for another 15 minutes.
Bake (High Heat is Key):
Oven: Preheat oven to maximum temperature (260 degrees+) with a pizza stone inside. Bake for 3–5 minutes until puffed.
Stovetop: Heat a cast-iron skillet on high. Cook for 1 minute, flip, cook another minute, and flip again until it puffs.

Prepare: Preheat oven to 350°F (180°C). Grease a 9-inch pan with tahini or ghee.
Make Batter: Mix dry ingredients, add ghee and mix until sandy. Add yogurt and milk (if needed) until a thick batter forms.
Bake: Spread into the pan, cut into diamond shapes, and place an almond on each. Bake for 30-40 minutes.
Syrup & Soak: Boil syrup for 5-10 mins. Pour the hot syrup over the hot cake immediately after baking.
Serve: Let it cool to room temperature before serving for the best texture.

Sauté Meat & Aromatics: Heat olive oil in a large pot. Brown the meat (lamb or chicken) on all sides. Add the onion and sauté until translucent.
Add Spices & Tomato: Add the tomato paste, turmeric, chili powder, and spices. Sauté for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Simmer: Add the garlic, boiling water/broth, and chili peppers (optional). Cover and simmer for 40–60 minutes (chicken) or up to 1.5 hours (lamb) until the meat is tender.
Cook Pasta: Bring the sauce back to a rapid boil. Add the pasta. Stir frequently to prevent sticking.
Finish: Cook for 10–15 minutes (or according to package instructions) until the pasta is cooked through but the sauce remains soupy.
Serve: Garnish with fresh basil or parsley and a squeeze of lemon juice.

Sauté Meat & Aromatics: Heat olive oil in a large pot. Brown the meat (lamb or chicken) on all sides. Add the onion and sauté until translucent.
Add Spices & Tomato: Add the tomato paste, turmeric, chili powder, and spices. Sauté for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Simmer: Add the garlic, boiling water/broth, and chili peppers (optional). Cover and simmer for 40–60 minutes (chicken) or up to 1.5 hours (lamb) until the meat is tender.
Cook Pasta: Bring the sauce back to a rapid boil. Add the pasta. Stir frequently to prevent sticking.
Finish: Cook for 10–15 minutes (or according to package instructions) until the pasta is cooked through but the sauce remains soupy.
Serve: Garnish with fresh basil or parsley and a squeeze of lemon juice.

Prepare the Syrup: In a saucepan, combine sugar and water. Bring to a boil, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Add lemon juice and simmer for 10-15 minutes until it slightly thickens. Stir in the rosewater/orange blossom water, then set aside to cool completely.
Prepare the Filling: Mix the ground almonds, grated marzipan, cinnamon (if using), and orange blossom water. It should feel slightly moist but not wet.
Prepare the Pan: Brush a large, deep rectangular baking pan (e.g., 9x13 inch) generously with melted ghee.
Layer the Pastry:
Lay one sheet of filo in the tray and brush generously with melted ghee. Repeat until you have about 15–20 layers, brushing every single sheet.
Spread the nut filling evenly over the bottom layers.
Cover with the remaining filo sheets, repeating the process of brushing ghee between every layer.
Cut and Bake:
Using a sharp knife, cut the baklawa into diamonds or squares.
Pour any remaining melted ghee over the top, making sure it gets into the cut lines.
Bake at 180°C (350°F) on the lower-middle shelf for 1 hour to 1 hour 15 minutes, or until golden brown and flaky.
Add Syrup: Immediately upon removing the baklawa from the oven, pour the cold syrup over the hot pastry.
Cooling: Allow to cool at room temperature for at least 8 hours or overnight before serving. This allows the syrup to soak in properly.

Prepare the Meat: Ensure the meat and fat are very cold. Grind the meat and fat together using a fine cutting die.
Season the Mixture: In a large bowl, combine the minced meat with all the spices, garlic, harissa, tomato paste, fresh herbs, and salt.
Mix Well: Thoroughly mix the ingredients, adding the ice-cold water gradually until the mixture becomes sticky.
Marinate: Cover the mixture and refrigerate overnight. This allows the spices to penetrate the meat.
Stuff the Casings: Load the meat into a sausage stuffer fitted with a small (narrow) nozzle. Stuff the sheep casings, ensuring they are not too tightly packed to avoid bursting.
Link the Sausages: Twist the stuffed casing into 4-5 inch links.
Cook: Grill over hot charcoal or pan-fry in a little olive oil for about 5-8 minutes until cooked through

Prepare the Lamb: Trim any excessive fat from the lamb shoulder or leg. Using a sharp knife, make deep incisions (cuts) all over the lamb to allow the marinade to penetrate.
Make the Marinade: In a bowl, combine the softened butter, garlic, ground cumin, coriander, paprika, turmeric, cinnamon, salt, pepper, and olive oil (if using) into a paste.
Rub and Marinate: Rub the paste thoroughly over the lamb, ensuring it gets inside the cuts. Tip: For the best flavor, cover and refrigerate for 4 hours or overnight.
Preheat and Prepare: Preheat the oven to 160°C (320°F/Gas 3).
Roast: Place the lamb in a large roasting pan and pour the water or broth into the bottom of the pan. Cover the pan very tightly with heavy-duty aluminum foil to create a steam trap.
Slow Cook: Roast for 4–5 hours. The lamb is ready when it is "meltingly tender" and comes away easily from the bone. Baste with the pan juices every hour, resealing the foil each time.
Brown the Skin: Remove the foil and turn the oven to 220°C (425°F/Gas 7). Roast for another 20-30 minutes, basting occasionally, until the outside is golden brown and crispy.
Rest and Serve: Allow the lamb to rest for at least 15-20 minutes before pulling apart or slicing.

Sauté Aromatics: Heat olive oil in a tagine or large pot over medium heat. Add onion and garlic, cooking until softened.
Add Spices: Add ginger, cumin, turmeric, cinnamon, and salt. Stir for 1 minute until fragrant.
Simmer Vegetables: Add carrots, sweet potatoes, zucchini, tomatoes (with juices), broth, and dried fruit. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low.
Slow Cook: Cover and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until vegetables are tender.
Finish: Stir in the chickpeas and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Serve: Garnish with fresh parsley or cilantro and a squeeze of fresh lemon juice. Serve hot with couscous or crusty bread.

Marinate the Chicken (Optional but Recommended): In a large bowl, combine the garlic, spices (ginger, turmeric, pepper, salt, saffron), half the herbs (parsley/cilantro), and a tablespoon of olive oil. Rub this mixture over the chicken pieces and let it sit for at least 1 hour, or overnight in the fridge.
Sauté Onions: Heat the olive oil in a tagine or heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the chopped onions and cook until soft and translucent.
Brown the Chicken: Add the chicken pieces to the pot, searing them on all sides until golden brown.
Simmer: Add the rest of the herbs and just enough water or stock to come halfway up the chicken (about 1-2 cups). Bring to a simmer, reduce heat to low, cover the tagine, and cook for 45-60 minutes, or until the chicken is tender.
Add Olives and Lemon: Add the green olives and the rinds of the preserved lemons to the pot.
Reduce Sauce: Remove the lid and continue to cook on low heat for another 10-15 minutes, allowing the sauce to reduce and thicken.
Serve: Serve directly in the tagine with crusty bread (Khobz) for dipping.

Sear Meat & Sauté Veg: In a large pot, heat 2 tbsp olive oil (or smen - fermented butter) on medium-high. Sear the meat until browned. Add onions and celery, cooking until softened (5–7 mins).
Add Spices & Tomatoes: Add tomato paste, ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, black pepper, and salt. Stir for 1-2 minutes until fragrant. Add the blended tomatoes, parsley, and cilantro.
Simmer: Add chickpeas, lentils, and enough water (approx. 6–8 cups) to cover ingredients generously. Bring to a boil, then cover and simmer on low for 45-60 minutes, or until the meat and lentils are tender.
Thicken the Soup: Increase heat to medium. While stirring continuously to avoid clumping, pour in the flour/water mixture (tedouira).
Add Pasta & Finish: Add the vermicelli. Cook for another 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the pasta is cooked and the soup has a thick consistency.
Serve: Serve hot with lemon wedges for squeezing.

Prep the Eggplants (Two Methods)
Method A (Roasting/Broiling - Recommended for smoky flavor): Halve the eggplants, prick with a fork, and place skin-side up under a broiler for 20–25 minutes until charred and very soft. Scoop out the flesh.
Method B (Stovetop - Quick method): Peel and chop the eggplants into 1-inch cubes. Sauté with garlic and oil in a pan until softened. 
2. Cook the Tomatoes and Spices
In a large skillet, combine the chopped/grated tomatoes, minced garlic, spices (cumin, paprika), salt, herbs (cilantro, parsley), and olive oil.
Cook over medium heat until the tomatoes break down and form a rich sauce, about 10–15 minutes.  Combine and Mash
Add the cooked eggplant to the tomato sauce. Use a potato masher or wooden spoon to mash the mixture together until you reach a creamy yet slightly chunky consistency.
Simmer on low for 10–15 minutes to allow the flavors to blend, ensuring most liquid has evaporated. 
4. Finish and Serve
Stir in the fresh lemon juice, if using.
Serve warm or at room temperature, drizzled with olive oil and garnished with more fresh parsley or cilantro

Roast the Peppers: Roast the green peppers over an open gas flame or under a broiler until the skin is charred and blackened all over.
Steam and Peel: Place the roasted peppers in a bowl and cover with plastic wrap for 10 minutes (this makes them easier to peel). Once cooled, peel off the charred skin, remove the seeds, and chop the flesh into small pieces.
Prepare the Base: In a large skillet, heat the olive oil over medium-low heat. Add the minced garlic and sauté for 1 minute until fragrant.
Cook the Tomatoes: Add the diced/grated tomatoes, paprika, cumin, cayenne (if using), and salt. Cook for about 10 minutes until the tomatoes soften and turn into a thick sauce.
Simmer Together: Add the chopped roasted peppers, parsley, and cilantro to the tomato sauce. Mix well, cover, and let it simmer for another 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally and mashing the vegetables with a spoon until the mixture is thick and very little liquid remains.
Serve: Remove from heat and serve warm or cold with bread.

Activate Yeast: Mix the yeast, sugar, and a few tablespoons of warm water in a small bowl. Let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy.
Make the Dough: In a large bowl, combine flour, salt, and yeast mixture. Gradually add the remaining lukewarm water, mixing vigorously by hand. The dough must be very sticky and soft—almost a thick batter consistency.
Rise: Cover the bowl with cling film and let it rise in a warm place for 3-4 hours, until it triples in size.
Shape and Fry:
Heat about 2.5 inches of oil in a deep pot to 180 degrees.
Keep a small bowl of water nearby. Wet your hands to handle the dough, pull off a piece, make a hole in the center, and stretch into a ring.
Carefully drop the dough into the hot oil. Fry for 1-2 minutes per side until deep golden brown.
Drain and Serve: Remove from the oil and drain on paper towels. Serve hot, optionally rolled in sugar.

Prep the Grains: Place couscous in a large bowl. Add 1 tbsp olive oil and salt, rubbing the grains to coat. Add 
cup of water and break up any lumps with your hands, allowing it to rest for 5 minutes.
First Steam: Bring the lower part of the couscoussier (pot) filled with meat, onions, spices, and water to a boil. Place the couscous in the upper steamer basket. Steam for 20 minutes from the time steam rises through the grains.
Hydrate and Second Steam: Pour the couscous back into the bowl. Drizzle with water and break up lumps. Re-steam for another 15 minutes.
Final Steam and Finish: Repeat step 3. In the final steaming, the couscous should be tender. Add smen (if using) and fluff.
Assemble: Mound the couscous on a large platter, create a well, fill with meat and vegetables, and pour broth over everything

Sauté Aromatics: Sauté onions and peppers in olive oil until soft, then stir in garlic and spices, cooking until fragrant.
Make the Sauce: Add tomatoes and simmer for 10–15 minutes until the sauce thickens; adjust seasoning to taste.
Poach the Eggs: Create 4–6 wells in the mixture, cracking an egg into each.
Set the Eggs: Cover and cook on low for 5–8 minutes, until whites are set and yolks remain runny.
Serve: Top with herbs and serve immediately with crusty bread.

Prepare the Chickpeas: If using dried chickpeas, soak them in water overnight. Drain and rinse, then place in a large pot with 6-8 cups of fresh water (and a pinch of baking soda if desired for extra softness). Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer until very tender (1-1.5 hours). If using canned, simply simmer in their liquid + 2 cups water for 15-20 minutes.
Make the Flavor Base: While chickpeas simmer, smash or mince garlic. In a bowl, mix the garlic with the harissa, cumin, and olive oil to create a paste.
Combine and Season: When chickpeas are tender, stir the garlic-harissa paste, lemon juice, and salt to taste into the pot. Let it simmer for another 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors to blend.
Prepare the Bread: Tear or chop the crusty bread into bite-sized pieces. Place a generous amount of bread at the bottom of each serving bowl.
Assemble and Serve: Ladle the hot chickpea stew directly over the bread, ensuring it is very soupy so the bread absorbs the broth.
Garnish: Top immediately with optional additions like a poached egg, a drizzle of olive oil, more harissa, capers, and fresh parsley.

Prep the Base: Heat olive oil in a pan, add sliced garlic, diced onions, and minced peppers, sautéing until softened.
Add Flavor: Stir in the harissa paste, tomato paste (if using), and spices (caraway, coriander).
Simmer Sauce: Add chopped tomatoes and about 1/2 cup of water, simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes until the sauce thickens.
Cook Protein: Add merguez sausages (pre-grilled/sliced) and simmer until cooked through.
Add Eggs: Make small wells in the sauce and crack the eggs into them. Season the eggs with a pinch of salt.
Final Cook: Cover the pan with a lid and cook for 3-5 minutes, until the egg whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Serve: Garnish with fresh parsley and drizzle with olive oil, serving hot with crusty bread

Prep the Filling: Boil and mash the potato. In a bowl, mix mashed potatoes with tuna, chopped parsley, capers, onion, and spices.
Prepare the Pastry: Lay a sheet of filo pastry flat. Fold it in half to make a semicircle or into a square shape,, ensuring your hands are dry.
Create the Ring: Place a spoonful of the potato mixture onto the center of the pastry, forming a small ring or well to hold the egg.
Add the Egg: Carefully crack one egg into the center of the filling ring.
Fold & Seal: Immediately fold the edges of the pastry over the filling to form a triangle or rectangle. Secure the edges well.
Fry: Slide the brik into shallow, hot oil on medium heat. Fry for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until golden brown.
Drain & Serve: Remove with a slotted spoon and place on paper towels to drain excess oil. Serve immediately with lemon wedges

Prep Dough & Rest: Mix semolina, salt, and melted butter/oil. Gradually add water and orange blossom water, mixing until a dough forms. Important: Let the dough rest for at least 30 minutes to an hour to absorb liquids.
Make Filling: Mix date paste with cinnamon, oil, and orange blossom water, then form into long, thin logs.
Assemble Logs: Take a portion of the dough, flatten into a rectangle, and place a date log in the center. Fold the dough over the filling to cover, and seal, creating a long stuffed rope.
Cut & Shape: Flatten the top of the log slightly. Use a traditional Makroudh mold (or a knife) to decorate the top and cut into diamonds.
Cook (Fry or Bake):
Fry: Deep fry in hot oil (175 degrees celcius) until golden brown.
Bake: Bake in a preheated oven (180 degrees celcius) for 15-20 minutes until golden.
Soak in Syrup: Immediately upon removing from the oil (or oven), dip the hot cookies into lukewarm honey syrup for a few minutes.
Serve: Let them cool completely to allow the syrup to set.

Prep and Soak: If using dried Eru, soak in hot water for 15–30 minutes to soften. Rinse thoroughly and drain.
Cook Meat: Season beef, cow skin, and tripe with salt, onion, and seasoning cubes. Boil with very little water until tender.
Add Fish and Waterleaf: Add the smoked fish and chopped waterleaf/spinach to the pot. Let it cook for 5–10 minutes until the greens shrink.
Add Eru and Palm Oil: Add the soaked, drained Eru and palm oil. Mix thoroughly.
Simmer and Season: Add ground crayfish, extra seasoning cubes, and pepper. Stir well and let it simmer on low heat for another 10–15 minutes until the oil is well blended and the water evaporates.
Serve: Serve with Water Fufu (or Garri).

Prep Bitter Leaves: Wash leaves thoroughly (rubbing between palms) 3-4 times to remove bitterness. Boil for 15-20 minutes with a teaspoon of bicarbonate soda to soften and remove remaining bitterness, then rinse in cold water and squeeze dry.
Prep Peanuts & Spices: Boil peanuts for 10-15 minutes, drain, and blend with garlic, ginger, leeks, and half an onion into a slightly rough paste.
Cook Protein: Boil beef/cow skin with onion, salt, and seasoning cubes until tender.
Cook the Stew: Heat a small amount of oil in a large pot, add the peanut paste and some water, then cook for 15-20 minutes, stirring constantly to prevent sticking. Add ground crayfish, white pepper, and cooked meat with its stock.
Combine: Add the prepared bitter leaves to the peanut sauce and simmer on low heat for 10 minutes.
Finish: In a separate pan, sauté chopped onions and prawns in oil until cooked. Pour this mixture (oil and all) into the main pot, stir, and simmer for 5 more minutes.
Serve: Serve hot with miondo (fermented cassava sticks), plantains, or yam

Prep the Beans: Soak peeled black-eyed peas in water for 1–2 hours to soften.
Blend: Blend the soaked beans with peppers and about 1 cup of water to a slightly grainy, smooth consistency.
Mix & Aerate: Pour into a large bowl. Whisk vigorously (to incorporate air) while gradually adding heated palm oil.
Season: Add seasoning cubes (Maggie), salt, and chopped spinach/taro leaves.
Wrap: Place a scoop of the mixture into softened banana leaves (heated over flame) or aluminum foil and wrap tightly into pouches.
Steam: Place the pouches in a pot lined with leaves or foil, adding 2–3 cups of water. Steam for 1.5 to 2 hours, adding more water as needed to prevent burning.
Serve: Let it cool slightly to firm up before serving.

Prepare the Meat: Season beef and cow skin with salt and seasoning cubes, add water, and boil until tender.
Prepare the Cocoyams: Wash and boil cocoyams until soft. Peel them and, while still warm, pound in a mortar or blend with a food processor until smooth and sticky. Mold into a bowl and set aside.
Prepare the Limestone Solution: Dissolve about 1 tablespoon of limestone/kangwa in a small amount of warm water.
Prepare the Yellow Soup:
Method A (Blender - Fast): In a blender, combine warmed palm oil, beef broth (about 1-2 cups), the dissolved limestone solution, and achu spices. Blend until it emulsifies into a creamy yellow sauce.
Method B (Traditional): Add warmed palm oil to a pot, add the limestone solution, and stir vigorously until it turns yellow. Add the broth and mix well (or pour between two pots to emulsify).
Finalize the Soup: Add the boiled meat into the yellow sauce and simmer briefly (optional).
Serving: Create a mound of pounded cocoyam (fufu) with a hole in the center, and pour the soup into the hole

Prepare the Palm Fruit Concentrate (Skip if using canned concentrate)
Wash the palm nuts thoroughly.
Boil them in a large pot for 30–40 minutes until they are soft.
Pound the nuts in a mortar to separate the pulp from the nuts (do not break the hard inner nuts).
Add hot water to the pounded mixture and strain through a sieve to extract the thick palm juice.
Pre-cook the Meats
While the nuts are boiling, season your meats with salt, a chopped onion, and seasoning cubes.
Cook until tender and set aside, reserving the meat stock.
Cook the Soup Base
Pour the palm nut extract into a large pot and bring to a boil on medium heat.
Let it boil for 10-15 minutes until the oil starts to float to the top and the soup thickens.
Add the cooked meat, smoked fish, chopped onions, and Scotch bonnet peppers.
Add Spices and Seasoning
Add the crayfish, Banga spices, and the Oburunbebe stick.
Pour in the reserved meat stock.
Simmer for another 10–15 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent burning.
Final Touches
Add the chopped leafy greens (spinach or bitterleaf).
Taste and adjust seasoning with salt or more Maggi.
Allow to simmer for another 2–5 minutes until the soup is thick and aromatic.
Remove the Oburunbebe stick before serving.

Prepare the Meats and Stock: Wash all meats and place them in a large pot. Season with half the onions, seasoning cubes, and salt. Cook until tender. Add the stockfish and dried fish during the last 10 minutes of cooking to ensure they are soft. Set aside.
Make the Egusi Paste: In a small bowl, mix the ground egusi with water to create a thick, creamy paste. Some variations include stirring a small amount of onion into this paste.
Heat Oil and Fry Onions: Heat the palm oil in a large pot over medium heat for about 1 minute (do not bleach). Add the remaining onions and locust beans, frying for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Add Egusi: Lower the heat. Add the egusi paste into the oil. Fry the mixture for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent burning. The egusi will turn yellow and form small lumps (cakes) as the oil separates.
Combine Ingredients: Add the blended pepper, crayfish, and the cooked meat/fish with its stock. Stir well, but carefully so you don't break up the lumps completely.
Simmer: Cover the pot and let the soup simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes, allowing the flavors to blend. If the soup is too thick, add small amounts of water until you reach your desired consistency.
Add Vegetables: Add the washed and chopped vegetables (spinach or Ugu). Stir and let it simmer for another 3-5 minutes until the leaves are cooked.
Finish: Taste and adjust salt or seasoning if needed. The soup is ready when the palm oil floats to the top.

Peel the Plantains: Cut off both ends of the plantains. Make a shallow, lengthwise slit along the ridges of the peel, being careful not to cut into the flesh. Peel the skin away.
Slice: Slice the plantains diagonally into pieces about 1/2-inch thick. A diagonal cut offers more surface area for caramelization.
Heat the Oil: Heat about 1/4 inch of oil in a large skillet or frying pan over medium heat.
Fry in Batches: Once the oil is hot and shimmering, carefully add the plantain slices in a single layer. Do not overcrowd the pan, as this makes them soggy.
Flip: Fry for 2-3 minutes, or until the bottom is a deep golden brown. Gently flip using a slotted spoon or spatula and fry the other side for another 2-3 minutes.
Drain: Remove the plantains and place them on a paper towel-lined plate to drain excess oil.
Season and Serve: Sprinkle with a small pinch of salt while still warm to balance the sweetness. Serve immediately.

Prepare the Smoked Fish: Wash the smoked fish thoroughly with hot water to remove any soot or dirt. Remove the head, fins, and bones. Break the fish into large, bite-sized chunks.
Sauté Aromatics: Heat the oil in a large pot over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onions and fry until they are soft and translucent (about 5 minutes).
Add Tomatoes and Paste: Add the blended fresh tomatoes, tomato paste, garlic, and chopped chili peppers to the pot. Stir well. Cook for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomato mixture is dark and the oil begins to separate from the tomato mixture.
Add Seasoning: Add the bouillon cubes (Maggie), salt, and optional curry powder. Stir to combine.
Add Fish: Gently add the cleaned smoked fish pieces to the sauce. Stir carefully to avoid breaking the fish. Reduce the heat to low.
Simmer: Add a small amount of water to achieve the desired consistency. Cover the pot and let the stew simmer for 15-20 minutes, allowing the flavors to penetrate the fish.
Final Touch: Add the scent leaves or parsley (if using) and simmer for another 2-3 minutes.
Serve: Serve hot with fufu, rice, yam, or plantains

Prepare the Groundnuts: If you have raw groundnuts, roast them lightly in a dry pan until fragrant. Let them cool and remove the husks. Grind the groundnuts into a smooth, buttery paste using a blender or food processor, or traditionally, a mortar and pestle.
Sauté Onions and Tomatoes: In a saucepan, add a small amount of oil (optional, as peanuts have oil) and sauté the onions until soft. Add the diced tomatoes and cook until they break down into a paste (about 5-10 minutes).
Create the Base: Pour the groundnut paste into the pan with the onions and tomatoes. Stir continuously to combine.
Add Water and Simmer: Slowly stir in the hot water to prevent lumps, creating a smooth, creamy sauce. Bring the mixture to a gentle boil, then lower the heat to a simmer.
Cook Down: Simmer uncovered for 20-30 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent the sauce from sticking to the bottom, until it thickens and oil begins to separate and rise to the surface.
Season and Serve: Add salt to taste.

Wash the Cocoyam: Thoroughly wash the cocoyam tubers to remove all dirt. You can peel them before boiling or leave the skin on for better texture.
Boil: Place the cocoyams in a large pot and cover with enough water. Add salt to taste.
Cook Until Tender: Bring to a boil and cook for roughly 20–30 minutes until soft (test by piercing with a knife or fork).
Drain and Peel: Drain the water. If you boiled them with the skin on, peel the skin off now using a knife.
Serve: Serve hot. It is commonly served with palm oil, fresh pepper sauce, or garden egg sauce.

Prepare and Season the Meat: Wash the goat meat with water and a splash of white vinegar or lemon juice. Pat dry. In a large bowl, season the meat with half of the garlic, half of the ginger, one bouillon cube, and salt.
Sear the Meat: Heat oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high heat. Add the meat in batches, searing until browned on all sides. Remove the meat and set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: In the same pot, add the chopped onion, remaining garlic, ginger, and scotch bonnet peppers. Sauté for about 5 minutes until soft and fragrant.
Cook the Tomato Base: Add the tomato paste and grated tomatoes to the onions. Stir well and let it cook for 5–10 minutes, allowing the tomatoes to reduce and the oil to separate (this removes the raw tomato taste).
Simmer the Stew: Return the seared goat meat to the pot. Stir to coat the meat with the tomato mixture. Add spices (cumin, coriander, black pepper) and the remaining bouillon cube. Pour in water or stock to just cover the meat.
Slow Cook: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and let it simmer for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until the meat is very tender.
Finish and Serve: Taste the sauce and adjust the salt if necessary. If the sauce is too thin, simmer uncovered for the last 10 minutes to thicken. Garnish with chopped fresh herbs.

Prep Vegetables: Wash and chop all vegetables. Cube the sweet potatoes and eggplant; slice the okra.
Boil Base: In a large pot, combine the sweet potatoes, eggplant, onion, tomatoes, and stock/water. Add seasoning (bouillon, salt, chili).
Simmer: Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to a simmer. Cook for about 20–30 minutes until the vegetables are tender.
Add Okra and Greens: Add the okra and leafy greens to the pot and cook for another 5–10 minutes.
Add Peanut Butter: In a small bowl, mix the peanut butter with a few tablespoons of hot water or hot broth to create a smooth paste.
Thicken: Stir the peanut butter mixture into the main pot. Let it simmer for another 5–10 minutes until the stew is thickened and creamy.
Serve: Serve hot over white rice, millet, or boiled cassava.

Prepare the Cassava Leaves (Pondu): If using frozen leaves, allow them to thaw. Wash the crushed leaves thoroughly in a colander under tap water to remove bitterness and drain.
Initial Boiling (Toxicity Removal): Place the leaves in a large pot and cover with enough water. Boil for roughly 30 minutes to 1 hour, stirring occasionally. This step is crucial to remove any potential toxins and bitterness.
Prepare Aromatics: While the leaves boil, mix the onions, leeks, garlic, and green pepper in a blender or food processor until smooth or finely chopped.
Add Aromatics & Palm Oil: Once the leaves have simmered, add the blended aromatics (onions, leeks, garlic, green pepper) to the pot. Stir in the palm oil.
Simmer & Season: Add bouillon cubes, salt to taste, and the whole scotch bonnet pepper (if using). If using eggplant, add it now. Add more water if the pot looks too dry.
Slow Cook: Lower the heat and let the stew simmer for 1–2 hours. The goal is to evaporate the water while keeping the mixture moist, allowing the leaves to become soft and tender.
Add Fish: Add the sardines or mackerel during the last 10–15 minutes of cooking. Stir gently to avoid breaking the fish too much.
Final Adjustments: Taste and add more salt or bouillon if needed. The Pondu is ready when the water is mostly evaporated, the oil is separated, and the leaves are very tender.
Serve: Serve hot with white rice, kwanga (cassava bread), or fufu.

Prepare the Banana Leaves: Clean the banana leaves. To make them pliable and prevent tearing, quickly pass them over an open flame until they become shiny and flexible, or blanch them in hot water.
Marinate the Fish: In a bowl, combine the fish fillets with the onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, spring onions, hot peppers, nutmeg, oil, and bouillon cubes. Rub the marinade well into the fish and let it sit for at least 15-30 minutes.
Assemble the Liboké: Lay down two banana leaves in a cross shape. Place the fish mixture in the center. If you do not have banana leaves, you can use parchment paper covered with aluminum foil.
Wrap the Packets: Fold the leaves tightly to create a sealed "packet" or "burrito" shape, ensuring no liquids can leak out. Secure the package by tying it with strings or strips of banana leaf.
Cook the Liboké:
Oven: Bake at 350°F (180°C) for 30–45 minutes.
Steam/Stove: Place a small amount of water in a deep pot, place the packets inside, and steam for about 20–30 minutes.
Serve: Serve the Liboké hot, traditionally wrapped in the leaf, alongside boiled plantains, fufu, or rice.

Prep the Meat: If the bushmeat is smoked or dry, wash it thoroughly with hot water and salt to remove dirt and soot.
Season and Parboil: Place the meat in a pot. Add sliced onions, blended ginger/garlic/peppers, salt, and seasoning cubes. Add a little water, cover tightly, and simmer until tender. Note: If using a pressure cooker, this saves time.
Prepare the Sauce Base: In a separate large pot, heat the palm oil. Fry finely chopped onions, then add the tomato puree/crushed tomatoes. Fry on medium heat for 10-15 minutes until the acidity is reduced and the oil separates.
Combine: Pour the cooked bushmeat along with its stock into the stew base.
Simmer and Thicken: If you are using peanut butter or Egusi, stir it in now to thicken the stew. Add more seasoning to taste. Cover and let the stew simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the sauce to thicken.
Serve: Serve hot with fufu, kwanga (cassava bread), or boiled plantains.

Prepare the Leaves: If using frozen leaves, thaw them and wash thoroughly to remove any bitterness. Squeeze out excess liquid.
Blend Aromatics: In a food processor, blend the onions, garlic, green onions, leeks, and green peppers until a smooth paste is formed.
Initial Boil: In a large pot, combine the cassava leaves and the blended mixture. Add enough water to cover the leaves and bring to a boil on high heat for 30 minutes.
Add Oil and Simmer: Pour in the palm oil and add salt. Turn the heat down to medium, allowing it to simmer for another 30–60 minutes. Stir occasionally to prevent burning.
Add Protein & Heat: Add the smoked fish (or sardines) and the whole habanero pepper. If the mixture is too dry, add a small amount of water. Continue to simmer until the oil separates from the leaves and the stew is thick (about 30-45 more minutes).
Finish: Taste and adjust salt. Serve hot with fufu (cassava dough), rice, or plantains.

Prepare the Mbika Flour: Grind the roasted mbika/pumpkin seeds in a blender or food processor until they form a fine powder/flour.
Make the Seasoning Base: In a skillet, heat 1 tbsp of palm oil and sauté the onions, garlic, and chili peppers until browned and fragrant.
Mix the Dough: In a large bowl, mix the ground mbika powder, the sautéed onion/chili mixture, parsley, thyme, and stock cube. Gradually add warm water while mixing to form a thick, consistent paste. Add 2 tbsp of oil to the mixture to enhance moisture.
Add Protein (Optional): Fold in smoked fish, steamed shrimp, or cooked meat.
Wrap the Packets: If using banana leaves, pass them over a hot flame for a few seconds to make them pliable. Place a generous spoonful of the mixture in the center of a leaf or a piece of foil. Fold it into a neat parcel and tie with cooking twine or fold the edges tightly to ensure no leaks.
Cook:
Bain-marie (Best): Place the packets in a deep baking tray, add hot water halfway up the sides, and bake at 160°C–180°C (300°F–350°F) for 1 hour.
Steaming: Steam the packets for 30–60 minutes until the mixture is firm.
Serve: Allow the packets to cool slightly. Serve hot or cold with kwanga, plantains, fufu, or bread

Prepare the Fish (Desalting): Soak the salted fish in water for at least 8 hours, or overnight, changing the water multiple times to remove excess salt. Rinse, descale, and clean the fish thoroughly, then remove bones.
Fry the Fish: Heat 150ml of oil in a large pan over medium-high heat. Fry the salted fish chunks until they are golden brown and crispy. Remove the fish and set aside on paper towels to drain.
Sauté Aromatics: In the same oil, sauté the chopped onions, garlic, and bay leaves until the onions are translucent.
Cook Vegetables: Add the zucchinis, cabbage, and bell peppers to the onions. Stir well and let them simmer for about 4–8 minutes until the vegetables are tender.
Combine and Simmer: Add the fried fish back into the pan with the vegetables. Add the bouillon cube and scotch bonnet pepper.
Finish: Stir carefully to avoid breaking the fish, cover the pan, and let it simmer on low heat for an additional 3–5 minutes to allow the flavors to combine.
Serve: Serve hot with boiled cassava, fufu, or white rice

Prep the Caterpillars: If dried, soak them in warm water for 10-15 minutes, then rinse thoroughly to remove dirt and impurities. If fresh, clean them by removing the gut contents if desired.
Initial Boil (Optional): Some recipes involve boiling them in salted water for 10 minutes to tenderize them before frying.
Dry/Fry: Heat 2-3 tablespoons of oil in a skillet or frying pan. Add the caterpillars and stir-fry until they are golden brown and crispy.
Add Flavorings: Add the chopped onions, garlic, and chilies to the caterpillars. Sauté until the onions are soft.
Add Tomatoes: If using tomatoes, add them now and cook for another 5-10 minutes until a light sauce forms, or leave them out for a drier, crunchier result.
Serve: Season with salt to taste and serve hot

Prepare the Protein: If using cow leg, boil until tender (2-3 hours). If using smoked fish, debone and clean it.
Sauté Aromatics: In a large pot, heat palm oil over medium heat. Add chopped onions, garlic, and fresh tomatoes. Sauté until the tomatoes are soft and the mixture breaks down into a sauce.
Create the Base: Add the smoked meat or fish to the pot, along with the bay leaves and nutmeg. Stir well to coat the proteins in the tomato base.
Add Odika: Gradually stir in the Odika powder, mixing thoroughly to ensure it dissolves and doesn't clump.
Simmer: Add enough water or broth to cover the mixture. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover, and let it simmer for 20–30 minutes. The stew will thicken considerably due to the Odika.
Final Seasoning: Taste and add salt or pepper as required.

Prepare the Leaves: If using dried Eru, soak in hot water for 10-15 minutes to soften. Finely chop the waterleaf or spinach.
Cook Proteins: Boil seasoned beef, cow skin, and tripe with salt and bouillon until tender. If using smoked fish, add it toward the end so it doesn't break up.
Add Waterleaf: Add the chopped waterleaf/spinach to the boiling, seasoned meat and allow it to simmer for 5–10 minutes until it wilts.
Add Eru and Oil: Add the soaked and drained Eru (Gnetum) and red palm oil to the mixture.
Simmer and Season: Stir well, add ground crayfish and hot pepper, and let it simmer for another 10-15 minutes until the leaves are tender and the oil is fully integrated.
Final Adjustments: Taste and adjust seasoning, ensuring the final dish is rich and moist but not watery.

Prepare the Chicken: Season chicken pieces with salt, pepper, and optional mustard. Sear in a skillet with oil until browned on all sides, then set aside.
Sauté Aromatics: In a large pot, sauté the onions and garlic in red palm oil until translucent.
Prepare the Sauce: Add the palm nut puree and water to the onions and bring to a simmer.
Simmer the Stew: Return the chicken to the pot, add bouillon cubes, and whole Scotch bonnet peppers.
Cook Down: Cover and simmer on low heat for 30–60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the chicken is tender and the oil separates from the sauce, indicating it is done.
Serve: Serve hot with boiled plantains, cassava, or rice
